# Intake Brief: Demo 6: Exploration -- Finding the Best Sounds

**Work Item:** TF-0MLXF84591PAUGAJ | **Type:** Epic | **Priority:** High

> Summary: Provide an automated exploration pipeline (`toneforge explore`) that sweeps large seed ranges for a recipe, ranks results by selectable analysis metrics, clusters and surfaces top candidates, and supports promoting selected results into the persistent Library.

Problem statement

Developers need a way to systematically discover high-quality sounds across the large procedural seed/parameter space. Manual trial-and-error over thousands of seeds is slow and non-repeatable; a reproducible exploration pipeline that ranks, clusters, and persists top results will surface useful sounds quickly and enable promotion into the library for reuse.

Users

- Primary: Developers and sound designers who want to find the best procedural results without manual search.
- Secondary: CI/automation that curates candidate sounds and product teams that will import curated sounds into the Library.

User stories

- As a developer, I want `toneforge explore --recipe <r> --seed-range 0:10000 --keep-top 50 --rank-by transient-density` to produce an index of the top results so I can inspect promising candidates without listening to thousands of files.
- As a sound designer, I want `toneforge explore mutate --seed 4821 --jitter 0.1 --count 20` to generate perceptual variations around a chosen seed, so I can quickly iterate on promising material.
- As a curator, I want `toneforge explore promote --id <result-id>` to import a candidate into the persistent Library (TF-0MLXF8A901W1Y176) with metadata and analysis attached, so it can be searched and regenerated deterministically.

Success criteria (measurable)

1. Default exploration runs on a local single-machine environment and completes a 10,000-seed sweep within an operational time budget (target: under 2 minutes, adjustable by resources).
2. The CLI supports ranking by at least these metrics: transient-density, spectral-centroid, RMS (loudness), attack-time; users can select 1–4 metrics per run.
3. Exploration outputs are persisted by default as promoted Library entries (integrated with TF-0MLXF8A901W1Y176) containing: WAV (or canonical audio storage), analysis JSON (metrics per result), and deterministic regeneration metadata (recipe + seed + rendered parameters).
4. Clustering groups results into 3–8 clusters and presents cluster summaries; the `--keep-top N` flag correctly preserves top results by selected ranking metric(s) and supports reproducible selection.
5. End-to-end commands exist and are covered by tests: sweep, mutate, ranking, clustering, and promote (unit + integration where possible).

Constraints

- Implementation should target a single-machine default execution model (no new infra), but design for future extension to parallel or distributed execution.
- Depend on Demo 4 (Audio Analysis: TF-0MLXF7OU807BWPG6) and Demo 5 (Classification: TF-0MLXF7XBI0J0H3P8) for metrics and analysis primitives.
- Persisting directly into the Library requires schema alignment and may need migration work on TF-0MLXF8A901W1Y176.
- Performance targets (10k seeds under ~2 minutes) depend on renderer determinism and per-render cost; provide configuration for worker parallelism and a sensible default rate-limit.

Existing state

- There is an intake stub for Demo 6 in the worklog (this epic). Demo 4 (analysis) and Demo 5 (classification) are existing work items and provide analysis primitives and classification types.
- The CLI contains existing commands and patterns (`tf generate`, `tf show`, `tf list`) that the explore subcommands should follow for flags, help text, exit codes, and `--json` compatibility.

Desired change (high-level)

- Add `toneforge explore` subcommands: `explore sweep`, `explore mutate`, `explore promote` (names TBD). Implement a sweep runner that iterates seeds, renders deterministic audio, runs analysis metrics, stores metrics, applies ranking and clustering, and surfaces top results.
- Persist results via the Library import API so promotion writes canonical audio + metadata into TF-0MLXF8A901W1Y176.
- Add tests: small-scale sweep tests (fast), integration test that runs a short sweep and validates metrics and persistence, and unit tests for ranking/clustering logic.

Related work

- TF-0MLXF7OU807BWPG6 — Demo 4: Audio Analysis (prerequisite) — provides analysis metrics (transient-density, spectral features) used for ranking.
- TF-0MLXF7XBI0J0H3P8 — Demo 5: Classification (prerequisite) — provides classification types and may be used for tagging/promotions.
- TF-0MLXF8A901W1Y176 — Library: persistent storage for curated sounds — target for `promote` integration.
- `EXPLORE_PRD.md` — product doc referenced by the Demo 6 stub (if present in repo).
- `docs/prd/DEMO_ROADMAP.md` — roadmap referencing Demo 6.

Open questions (need answers before implementation)

1. Confirm the default sweep-size/time budget: 10,000 seeds and target time under 2 minutes — is this strict or an aspirational target to be relaxed by config? (Recommended: configurable default = 10k, warn if budget not met.)
2. Promotion policy: should `promote` create Library entries only or also copy WAVs to a local `./exploration/` directory for manual inspection? (Recommended: both — persist canonical audio in Library and optionally write local exports with `--export`.)
3. Access & resource safety: should the explore command be rate-limited by default (concurrency cap) to avoid saturating CPU/memory on developer machines? (Recommended: yes — default concurrency = number of logical CPUs / 2.)

data.description:
# Demo 7: Library -- Structured Asset Storage

**Work Item:** TF-0MLXF8A901W1Y176 | **Type:** Epic | **Priority:** High

> **Summary:** Build a persistent, indexed Library module that stores curated sounds with metadata, supports attribute and similarity search, deterministic regeneration, and WAV export -- replacing the explore promote staging area with direct Library integration.

## Problem Statement

Promoted sounds are written to a flat `.exploration/promoted/` staging directory with no indexing, querying, or management. The Library module replaces this with structured, queryable storage that bridges exploration and production.

## Users

**Sound designers and developers** using ToneForge to build and manage collections of procedurally generated sounds for games, apps, or media projects.

### User Stories

- As a developer, I want `explore promote` to write directly to the Library so promoted sounds are immediately searchable and retrievable without a separate import step.
- As a developer, I want to list all Library entries by category so I can browse my curated sound collection.
- As a developer, I want to search the Library by attributes (intensity, texture, tags) so I can find sounds matching specific criteria.
- As a developer, I want to find sounds similar to a given Library entry so I can discover alternatives without manually auditioning every entry.
- As a developer, I want to export Library entries as WAV files organized by category so I can deliver them to a production pipeline.
- As a developer, I want to regenerate a Library entry from its stored preset so I can verify determinism or re-render with updated ToneForge code.

## Success Criteria

1. `explore promote` writes entries directly to the Library at `.toneforge-library/` in CWD, with entry ID format `lib-<candidateId>`, and the entry is immediately queryable via `library list` and `library search`.
2. `library list` displays entries filterable by category; `library search` returns entries matching attribute queries (intensity, texture, tags) across the Library index.
3. `library similar --id <id>` returns perceptually related entries using a hybrid approach: analysis-metric distance (RMS, spectral centroid, duration) as primary, with tag/label overlap as tiebreaker.
4. `library regenerate --id <id>` re-renders the sound from the stored preset (recipe + seed + params) and replaces the stored audio file.
5. `library export --category <c> --format wav --output <dir>` produces valid WAV files organized by category in the output directory.
6. All `library` CLI subcommands support `--json` for machine-readable output, consistent with existing ToneForge CLI conventions.
7. Promoting the same candidate twice is idempotent: the second promote returns the existing Library entry without creating a duplicate.

## Constraints

- **Scope is Demo 7 + promote integration only.** The full LIBRARY_PRD features (versioning/history, UI browser, metadata editor, cloud-backed libraries, 100k-entry scale) are explicitly out of scope.
- **Single JSON index file.** The Library index is a single JSON file loaded into memory. No SQLite or database dependency. Sufficient for hundreds to low thousands of entries.
- **WAV export only.** The export command produces WAV files only. No other audio format support in this scope.
- **No classification embeddings.** The classification module does not yet produce embedding vectors. Similarity search uses analysis metrics + tag overlap as a proxy. A separate work item (TF-0MM1F5KU61G3JYC0) tracks adding embedding vectors.
- **No backward compatibility for staging area.** The `.exploration/promoted/` staging directory is deprecated. Previously promoted entries must be re-promoted through the new flow.
- **Category defaults to classification data.** Entry category is derived from the classification result's category field, with an optional `--category` flag for user override.
- **Entry ID format:** `lib-<candidateId>` (prefixed candidateId from explore).
- **Storage location:** `.toneforge-library/` in CWD (hidden directory, consistent with `.exploration/` convention).

## Existing State

- **No Library module exists.** The entire `src/library/` directory is unbuilt.
- **Explore promote is a staging area.** `src/explore/promote.ts` writes WAV+JSON to `.exploration/promoted/` with placeholder `libraryId` values (`lib-${recipe}-${seed}`). It explicitly documents that "The Library (TF-0MLXF8A901W1Y176) is not yet implemented."
- **Promote metadata schema exists.** Promoted entries include: libraryId, candidateId, recipe, seed, duration, sampleRate, params, analysis, classification, score, metricScores, cluster, runId, promotedAt.
- **Persistence pattern established.** Explore's `persistence.ts` provides a file-system JSON pattern (save/load/list/getLatest) that can inform Library storage design.
- **LIBRARY_PRD.md provides full design.** The PRD defines entry schema, storage layout, tag system, search/query types, regeneration, and batch operations. Only a subset is needed for Demo 7.
- **Demo 6: Exploration (TF-0MLXF84591PAUGAJ) is completed.** The prerequisite is satisfied.

## Desired Change

### New module: `src/library/`

1. **Library Entry type** -- Define `LibraryEntry` with fields: id (`lib-<candidateId>`), recipe, seed, category, duration, tags, analysis metrics, classification labels, preset (recipe + seed + params), provenance (toneforgeVersion), file paths (WAV, metadata JSON), promotedAt timestamp.
2. **Library Index** -- Single JSON index file at `.toneforge-library/index.json` with all entry metadata. Loaded into memory for search and listing. Written back on mutation.
3. **Storage layer** -- Store WAV files and metadata JSON per entry in `.toneforge-library/<category>/` directories. Functions: addEntry, getEntry, listEntries, searchEntries, findSimilar, exportEntries, regenerateEntry.
4. **Similarity engine** -- Hybrid approach: compute distance using normalized analysis metrics (RMS, spectral centroid, duration, zero-crossing rate) as primary features, with tag/label Jaccard overlap as tiebreaker.
5. **CLI commands:**
   - `toneforge library list [--category <c>]` -- List entries, optionally filtered by category.
   - `toneforge library search [--intensity <i>] [--texture <t>] [--tags <t>]` -- Search by attributes.
   - `toneforge library similar --id <id> [--limit <n>]` -- Find similar entries.
   - `toneforge library export --category <c> --format wav --output <dir>` -- Export WAV files.
   - `toneforge library regenerate --id <id>` -- Re-render from preset, replace stored audio.

### Modified module: `src/explore/promote.ts`

6. **Direct Library integration** -- Change `promoteCandidate()` to write directly to the Library via the new Library module's `addEntry()` function instead of the `.exploration/promoted/` staging area. The promote result should contain the real `libraryId` from the Library.

## Risks & Assumptions

### Assumptions

- The classification module's category field provides a reasonable default category for Library entries. If classification data is absent (e.g., for entries promoted without classification), the user must supply `--category` explicitly.
- The existing promote metadata schema (candidateId, recipe, seed, params, analysis, classification) contains all data needed to construct a Library entry without re-analysis or re-classification.
- A single JSON index file performs adequately for the expected scale (hundreds to low thousands of entries). If the Library grows beyond this range, a migration to a more scalable index is a separate effort.
- Deterministic regeneration produces output consistent with the original render. If ToneForge code has changed since the original render, the regenerated audio may differ from the stored version; this is expected behavior for the "replace" mode.

### Risks

- **JSON index corruption or concurrent writes.** A single JSON index file has no locking mechanism. Concurrent CLI invocations (e.g., two promote operations in parallel) could corrupt the index. *Mitigation:* Document single-writer assumption; consider file-level advisory locking if concurrent use is anticipated.
- **Similarity quality without embeddings.** The hybrid metrics + tags approach may produce less meaningful similarity results than proper embedding-based search, especially for sounds that differ acoustically but share tags. *Mitigation:* Treat similarity as "best-effort" in this scope; the embedding work item (TF-0MM1F5KU61G3JYC0) provides the proper solution.
- **Promote integration breaking existing tests.** Changing `promoteCandidate()` to write to the Library instead of the staging area will break existing promote unit and integration tests. *Mitigation:* Update all affected tests as part of the promote integration sub-task.
- **Scope creep.** The LIBRARY_PRD describes many features beyond Demo 7 scope (versioning, history, UI browser, batch operations, cloud-backed libraries). *Mitigation:* Record additional feature ideas as separate work items linked to TF-0MLXF8A901W1Y176 rather than expanding this epic.
- **Missing classification data.** If a promoted candidate lacks classification results (e.g., from an older explore run), the entry cannot auto-derive a category or tags. *Mitigation:* Require either classification data or an explicit `--category` flag; fail with a clear error if neither is available.

## Related Work

- `docs/prd/LIBRARY_PRD.md` -- Full Library PRD. Sections 4-8 (entry schema, storage layout, indexing, search) are the primary design reference for Demo 7 scope.
- `docs/prd/DEMO_ROADMAP.md` (lines 349-407) -- Demo 7 specification with CLI examples and acceptance criteria.
- `docs/prd/EXPLORE_PRD.md` -- Explore PRD Section 8 references Library promotion.
- Demo 6: Exploration (TF-0MLXF84591PAUGAJ) -- **completed** prerequisite. Provides the promote staging area that this epic replaces.
- Promote Integration -- Library (TF-0MM0BWBNQ0X2R4DV) -- **completed** explore child. Implemented the staging-area promote. Will be superseded by this epic.
- Recipe Resolution & Library Integration (TF-0MM196HMG046T6LF) -- **blocked** Sequencer child. Needs Library to resolve libraryId -> recipeName. This epic unblocks it.
- Demo 8: Sequencer (TF-0MLXF8LCK12RDRJD) -- **blocked** on this epic. Needs Library for event references.
- Classification embedding vectors for similarity search (TF-0MM1F5KU61G3JYC0) -- **open**. Future improvement to replace the hybrid similarity approach with proper embedding-based search.

## Related work (automated report)

### Related work items

- **Enhance Library similarity search beyond metrics/tags** (TF-0MM1FA3Q21PONOH2, open) -- Child of this epic. Planned future enhancement to provide perceptual and embedding-based similarity, configurable backends, and CLI/API endpoints for discovery beyond the basic metrics+tags approach implemented in Demo 7.

- **Demo 11: Intelligence -- Assisted Decision-Making** (TF-0MLXF98WZ1LQGM5W, blocked) -- Depended-on-by (formal dependency). Intelligence audits and recommends from Library data. Its `intelligence audit --library` and `intelligence recommend` commands require the Library module to read entries, metadata, and classification data.

- **Demo 12: Compiler & Validator -- Production-Ready Assets** (TF-0MLXF9FWG18T2ZHD, blocked) -- Depended-on-by (formal dependency). The Compiler transforms curated Library content into platform-specific artifacts. Its `validate --library` and `compile --library` commands consume Library entries directly.

- **Demo 5: Classification -- Semantic Labels for Sounds** (TF-0MLXF7XBI0J0H3P8, completed) -- Upstream provider. Classification produces the category, intensity, texture, material, and tags data that Library entries store. The classification output schema defines the `classification` field structure within `LibraryEntry`. The `classify search` patterns also inform `library search` design.

- **Demo 4: Audio Analysis -- Measuring What You Made** (TF-0MLXF7OU807BWPG6, completed) -- Upstream provider. Analysis produces the RMS, spectral centroid, duration, and other metrics that Library stores per entry and uses as primary features in the similarity engine. The `AnalysisResult` type defines the `analysis` field within `LibraryEntry`.

- **Persistence & Result Index** (TF-0MM0BWHVX1XU5PEH, completed) -- Pattern reference. Explore's `persistence.ts` established the file-system JSON persistence pattern (save/load/list/generateId) that the Library index and storage layer should follow for consistency.

- **Clustering Engine** (TF-0MM0BW76416EQULD, completed) -- Design reference. The k-means clustering approach (metric normalization, distance computation, exemplar selection) informs the Library similarity engine's hybrid distance calculation.

- **CLI Classify Search** (TF-0MM0AMV9D0G8Y7CB, completed) -- Pattern reference. The classify search command's filter design (category exact match, intensity exact match, texture partial match, AND logic, `--dir` flag, table/JSON output) should be mirrored by `library search` for CLI consistency.

- **Add --latest flag to explore show and explore promote** (TF-0MM0HEPY817DF5GH, completed) -- Integration reference. The `--latest` flag and `getLatestRunId()` function in explore promote are used in the promote-to-Library flow. The promote CLI integration must continue to work with `--latest` when writing to the Library instead of the staging area.

- **Deterministic RNG & Seeding Policy** (TF-0MM196BQ90Z3AAT3, blocked) -- Related design concern. The seeding policy (baseSeed + seedOffset) is relevant to `library regenerate`, which must use the same seed to reproduce the original render deterministically.

- **Demo Walkthrough: Exploration** (TF-0MM0GD2XL1O4CLKR, completed) -- Will need updating. The exploration demo walkthrough shows the promote-to-staging-area flow that this epic replaces with direct Library integration. A new Library demo walkthrough should reference the updated promote behavior.

### Related repository documents

- **`docs/prd/LIBRARY_PRD.md`** -- Full PRD for ToneForge Library. Defines the complete architecture including entry schema, storage layout, indexing, search/query types, similarity, regeneration, versioning, and batch operations. Sections 4-8 are the primary design reference for Demo 7 scope; remaining features (versioning/history, UI browser, cloud-backed libraries) are out of scope.

- **`docs/prd/DEMO_ROADMAP.md`** (lines 349-407) -- Demo 7 specification. Defines goals, CLI commands (`library list`, `library search`, `library similar`, `library export`, `library regenerate`), key proof points, and acceptance criteria. Authoritative specification for what Demo 7 must deliver.

- **`docs/prd/EXPLORE_PRD.md`** -- Explore PRD. Section 8 references Library promotion (`promoteToLibrary`). Defines the promote workflow that Demo 7 replaces with direct Library integration.

- **`docs/prd/CLASSIFY_PRD.md`** -- Classify PRD. Defines the classification output schema (category, material, intensity, tags, classificationVersion) that Library entries store. Section 5 describes the `classify(buffer, analysisData, options)` API whose output becomes the Library entry's `classification` field.

- **`docs/prd/ANALYZE_PRD.md`** -- Analyze PRD. Defines the analysis output schema (duration, peak, RMS, spectral centroid, etc.) stored in Library entries and used as primary features in the similarity engine.

- **`docs/prd/COMPILER_PRD.md`** -- Compiler PRD. Describes how the Compiler consumes Library entries to produce platform-specific artifacts. Defines the Library-to-Compiler data contract and the `compile --library` workflow.

- **`docs/prd/VALIDATOR_PRD.md`** -- Validator PRD. Describes validation rules applied to Library entries before compilation. The `validate --library` command reads Library entries and checks peak clipping, duration bounds, and silence ratio.

- **`docs/prd/INTELLIGENCE_PRD.md`** -- Intelligence PRD. Describes how Intelligence reasons over Library data to provide audit, recommendation, and exploration-suggestion features. Defines the Library-to-Intelligence data contract.

- **`docs/prd/SEQUENCER_PRD.md`** -- Sequencer PRD. Describes how sequences reference Library entries by ID for event scheduling. Defines the `libraryId` reference format that the Library must support.

- **`docs/prd/CLI_PRD.md`** (Section 5.6) -- Defines the `library` CLI command interface (`toneforge library list`, `toneforge library export`). Establishes that `library` is a first-class command.

- **`docs/prd/MASS_GENERATION_PRD.md`** (Section 6) -- Defines the structured SFX Library entry schema, storage layout, and regeneration capability at full scale. The Demo 7 implementation is a subset of this design, limited to the single-JSON-index approach.

- **`docs/prd/PRD.md`** (Section 4.8) -- Defines ToneForge Library as the "System of Record" for all sound assets. Establishes Library as authoritative and immutable by default.
data.title: Demo 7: Library -- Structured Asset Storage

data.description:
Summary headline

Add a deterministic Sequencer that schedules event triggers from versioned JSON presets and exposes simulate/inspect/render CLI commands for reproducible temporal sound patterns (weapon bursts, melodies, rhythmic stings, ambient loops).

Problem statement

Move from single sound events to deterministic temporal patterns so game audio can express behavioral patterns (weapon bursts, melodies, rhythmic stings, ambient loops) as reusable, versioned presets. Implement a Sequencer that schedules event triggers (not audio) using JSON presets and deterministic seeds so timelines can be simulated, inspected, and rendered reproducibly.

Users

- Game developers and technical sound designers who author temporal behaviors (footsteps, weapon bursts, ambient patterns).
- Example user stories:
  - As a game developer, I want to run `toneforge sequence generate --preset presets/sequences/weapon_burst.json --seed 42 --duration 5s` to produce a deterministic weapon-fire event pattern rendered to audio so I can audition different weapon behaviors.
  - As a sound designer, I want `toneforge sequence simulate --preset presets/sequences/gameover_melody.json` to emit a JSON timeline (timestamps in ms) so I can verify timing and per-event seed offsets programmatically.
  - As a technical integrator, I want `toneforge sequence inspect --preset <file>` to show a human-readable event list so I can quickly review sequence structure.

Success criteria

- Determinism: Rendering the same preset+seed must produce byte-identical output across 10 consecutive runs in CI for the same environment and sample rate (44.1 kHz). Tests will assert byte-level equality across 10 runs.
- Timeline output: `sequence simulate` outputs a JSON timeline with event timestamps in milliseconds, event identifiers, and `seedOffset` values; timestamps must be accurate to within 1 sample at 44.1 kHz (≈0.023ms) when translating timeline→samples. Example timeline shape:

```json
{
  "events": [
    { "time_ms": 0, "event": "shot", "seedOffset": 0 },
    { "time_ms": 120, "event": "shot", "seedOffset": 1 }
  ],
  "sampleRate": 44100
}
```

- Presets: Provide at least three example presets: `weapon_burst.json`, `gameover_melody.json`, and `rhythmic_sting.json` under `presets/sequences/` using a documented versioned JSON schema.
- CLI surface: Implement `sequence generate`, `sequence simulate`, and `sequence inspect` with the flags described in the PRD (preset path, duration, seed, output optional for immediate playback when `--output` omitted).
- Tests & demo: Unit and integration tests for loader, simulator, and deterministic rendering; a demo walkthrough `demos/sequencer.md` that shows generate/simulate/inspect examples and plays audio locally.

Constraints

- Presets must be JSON files stored under `presets/sequences/` and include a `version` top-level field for forward compatibility.
- Sequencer schedules event triggers only; it must reference Stack/Recipe/Library items by name and not duplicate audio-generation logic.
- Use existing seeded RNG and deterministic rendering patterns used elsewhere in the repo (e.g., `createRng`) to guarantee repeatability.
- Sample rate for acceptance tests: 44.1 kHz. Timeline accuracy and determinism checks use this sample rate.
- Avoid introducing breaking CLI surface changes outside `toneforge sequence` commands.

Existing state

- PRD: `docs/prd/SEQUENCER_PRD.md` — conceptual definition, sequencing modes, runtime integration examples.
- Work item: TF-0MLXF8LCK12RDRJD (this intake) already contains a high-level description and initial acceptance criteria; status now `in-progress` and assigned to Map.
- Dependencies: Demo 3 (Sound Stacking) provides Stack/recipes used by sequences; Demo 7 (Library) supplies persistent library entries that sequences may reference.
- CLI patterns: The repository already includes CLI patterns and examples for `stack`, `generate`, and other demo commands that should be followed for `sequence` subcommands.

Desired change

- Implement a Sequencer feature that:
  1. Defines a stable, versioned JSON schema for sequence presets and places them under `presets/sequences/`.
  2. Adds `toneforge sequence generate|simulate|inspect` CLI commands following existing command registration patterns.
  3. Implements a simulator that converts preset → deterministic timeline (JSON) and a render path that schedules events and invokes Stack/Recipe rendering per event using seed + seedOffset.
  4. Ships three example presets and a `demos/sequencer.md` walkthrough.
  5. Adds unit and integration tests verifying loader validation, simulate timeline correctness, and determinism across repeated renders (10x equality).

Related work

- Potentially related docs:
  - `docs/prd/SEQUENCER_PRD.md` — conceptual PRD for the Sequencer and sequencing modes (repo path: `docs/prd/SEQUENCER_PRD.md`).
  - `docs/prd/DEMO_ROADMAP.md` — places Demo 8 in the demo sequence and lists dependencies (repo path: `docs/prd/DEMO_ROADMAP.md`).

- Potentially related work items:
  - `Demo 3: Sound Stacking -- Layered Events (TF-0MLXF7J4I0N0PKUW)` — Stack/recipe composition used by sequenced events.
  - `Library: persistent indexed storage (TF-0MLXF8A901W1Y176)` — optional target for event references (library IDs).
  - `Demo 9: Runtime & State (TF-0MLXF8TBT0DJDCO1)` — runtime integration and state-driven transitions (upstream consumer of Sequencer outputs).

Related work report

- Found matching work items in the worklog and repository: TF-0MLXF7J4I0N0PKUW (Demo 3), TF-0MLXF8A901W1Y176 (Library), TF-0MLXF8TBT0DJDCO1 (Demo 9), docs/prd/SEQUENCER_PRD.md. These are already referenced above and should be considered dependencies when implementing Sequencer.

Notes / Open questions

- Playback path: Should `sequence generate` behave like `stack render` and play audio immediately when `--output` is omitted? (Recommended: yes — follow `generate` behavior.)
- Seed offset semantics: Confirm whether `seedOffset` is an integer added to the base seed per event (recommended) or a per-event arbitrary seed (freeform).

Risks & mitigations

- Determinism risk: Platform or float-drift differences could make byte-level equality fragile across OS/architectures. Mitigation: run determinism tests in CI containers with pinned Node and sample-rate; document environment assumptions.
- Dependency risk: Sequencer depends on Stack/Recipes/Library being present. Mitigation: make the sequencer accept recipe references that can be stubbed during unit tests; record missing recipe errors as validation failures.
- Timeline accuracy risk: Converting timeline→samples may introduce rounding errors. Mitigation: require timeline timestamps be represented in integer sample offsets or ms with explicit conversion and test tolerance of 1 sample at 44.1 kHz.
- Scope creep risk: Consumers may request runtime features (state-machine integration, runtime API) during implementation. Mitigation: record additional requests as new work items linked to this epic, do not expand scope of this intake.

Files created by this draft

- `.opencode/tmp/intake-draft-Demo-8-Sequencer-TF-0MLXF8LCK12RDRJD.md` (this file)

Please review this draft and tell me any clarifications or edits. If this captures intent, reply "approve" and I will run the five intake reviews and finalize the work item description.
data.title: Demo 8: Sequencer -- Temporal Behavior Patterns

data.description:
Problem statement

Demonstrate and validate real-time, state-driven audio behavior by building a small demo harness (REPL or web page) that shows movement state transitions (walk → run → sprint) and environmental context (surface: stone/gravel) affecting runtime recipe/stack selection and playback parameters. The demo must be deterministic (seeded events) and provide inspectable logs and state history for verification.

Users

- Game audio engineers and interactive audio developers who need to verify runtime behavior and determinism (user story: "As a sound designer I can drive movement and surface state and hear immediate, deterministic footstep behavior so I can evaluate and tune recipes in context").
- Platform integrators and engine plugin authors who will validate runtime APIs and ensure the system can be embedded (user story: "As an integrator I can start the runtime and inspect state so I can map engine signals to ToneForge state/context").
- QA and infra engineers who will exercise determinism and performance (user story: "As QA I can replay a scripted event sequence and compare deterministic logs to confirm identical behavior across runs").

Success criteria

1. Runtime can be started/stopped from the demo (CLI or web) and responds to state/context changes in real time.
2. Movement transitions (walk → run → sprint) produce audible, timely sequence changes with no audible glitches; context changes (surface) switch recipe/stack selection within one event.
3. Determinism verification via logs: identical scripted command sequences with the same seeds produce identical deterministic logs and event ids across repeated runs.
4. Inspection API: `state.inspect()` (or equivalent) returns current values, last N transitions (default N=20), active sequences and active stacks.
5. Demo contains a README describing how it maps to `RUNTIME_PRD.md`, `STATE_PRD.md`, `CONTEXT_PRD.md`, how to run verification, and how to reproduce deterministic runs.

Constraints

- Follow demo conventions used elsewhere in the repo (demo CLI/repo path, storytelling format in `demos/README.md`).
- Keep the demo self-contained and lightweight; avoid full engine integrations (Unity/Unreal) and advanced spatial audio features.
- Use deterministic, seed-driven playback and log seeds and event ids; avoid platform-specific verification that may be brittle.

Existing state

- Sequencer capabilities are defined and required as a prerequisite (Demo 8). See `docs/prd/DEMO_ROADMAP.md` and `docs/prd/SEQUENCER_PRD.md`.
- Runtime, State, and Context PRDs exist and define APIs and expectations: `docs/prd/RUNTIME_PRD.md`, `docs/prd/STATE_PRD.md`, `docs/prd/CONTEXT_PRD.md`.
- The work item TF-0MLXF8TBT0DJDCO1 currently exists as the Demo 9 epic and has a high-level description and acceptance criteria; it will be updated with this intake brief.

Desired change

- Deliver a runnable demo harness under `demos/demo-9-runtime-state` (or according to repo demo conventions) containing:
  - minimal stacks/recipes for two surfaces (stone, gravel) and three movement states (walk, run, sprint), either checked into the demo folder or referenced from library entries;
  - demo code (REPL and/or lightweight web page consistent with other demos) exposing runtime start/stop, state/context setting, and an inspect API;
  - logging that emits deterministic event ids, timestamps, seeds, and transition records; exportable logs for verification;
  - a README that documents how to run the demo, expected commands, and determinism verification steps.

Related work

- docs/prd/RUNTIME_PRD.md — Runtime design and API (playback modes, determinism expectations).
- docs/prd/STATE_PRD.md — State machine model, parameters, and inspection API.
- docs/prd/CONTEXT_PRD.md — Context snapshot model and dimensions.
- docs/prd/DEMO_ROADMAP.md — Demo sequencing and conventions; Demo 8 (Sequencer) is a prerequisite.
- Worklog: Demo 8: Sequencer -- Temporal Behavior Patterns (TF-0MLXF8LCK12RDRJD) — prerequisite; ensure sequencing is available for runtime.
data.title: Demo 9: Runtime & State -- Real-Time Behavioral Sound

data.description:
Demo 10: Mixer — Intelligent Audio Balancing (TF-0MLXF8ZW21HJLFOG)

Headline
Implement a behavior-aware Mixer runtime that enforces deterministic mix groups, priority-based ducking/boosting, and voice-limiting using declarative rules stored in repo JSON files.

Problem statement
Under concurrent audio/visual load (footsteps, combat, UI, ambience, etc.) outputs can mask each other and create an unusable, noisy mix. We need a deterministic runtime Mixer that applies intent-aware prioritization, ducking/boosting, and voice limits so the system maintains clarity and intent without human mixing.

Users
- Game/audio integrators and developers who embed ToneForge into interactive systems and need predictable, low-latency arbitration of concurrent outputs.
- Example user stories:
  - As a game audio engineer, I want ambience to be automatically ducked when combat activates so critical sounds remain audible.
  - As a developer, I want per-group voice limits so the system never exceeds allowed concurrent sources and avoids CPU spikes.
  - As an accessibility engineer, I want deterministic rules so we can reliably tune priority behavior and test outcomes.

Success criteria (measurable)
- Mix groups implemented and configurable: footsteps, UI, combat, ambience, dialogue, alerts.
- Voice limiting enforced per group with default max voices = 4; tests confirm group never exceeds limit under load.
- State-driven duck/boost rules applied from declarative JSON; default duck depth = 6 dB and UI duck duration = 200 ms.
- Max end-to-end mixer decision latency ≤ 5 ms under target load (benchmarked in CI test harness).
- `mixer` behavior is deterministic and unit-testable: same input state & events → identical rule outcomes and gains.

Constraints
- Depends on Runtime and State (Demo 9) being available for the Mixer to observe state and for Runtime to apply gain/limits.
- Must be low-latency and deterministic; avoid complex ML inference in the runtime path (Intelligence can suggest rules but must not auto-apply them).
- Rules storage must be auditable and version-controlled in the repository.

Existing state
- PRD: `docs/prd/MIXER_PRD.md` contains an extensive product-level design and examples.
- Roadmap: `docs/prd/DEMO_ROADMAP.md` includes Demo 10 context and example `mixer.inspect()` interactions.
- Worklog: TF-0MLXF8ZW21HJLFOG exists (title: "Demo 10: Mixer -- Intelligent Audio Balancing") and currently in `idea` stage, `open` status, assignee `Map`.

Desired change
- Implement core Mixer runtime features:
  - Mix group abstraction (intent-based groups)
  - Priority-based rule engine applying duck/boost/limit actions
  - Voice limiting enforcement per group
  - Deterministic rule evaluation with unit tests and fixtures
- Provide declarative rule schema and example JSON rules stored in `config/mixer/*.json`.

Related work
- Potentially related docs:
  - `docs/prd/MIXER_PRD.md` — full Mixer PRD describing concepts, examples, and goals.
  - `docs/prd/DEMO_ROADMAP.md` — demo sequence and Demo 10 scenario showing expected interactions.
  - `docs/prd/BRAINSTORM_QUESTIONS.md` — design tradeoffs and concerns about Mixer responsibilities.

- Potentially related work items:
  - Demo 10: Mixer -- Intelligent Audio Balancing (TF-0MLXF8ZW21HJLFOG) — current work item (idea, open, assignee Map).
  - Demo 13: Visualizer & Haptics -- Cross-Modal Output (TF-0MLXF9O241QG5APK) — lists Demo 10 as a prerequisite; cross-modal alignment may be relevant later.

Notes & open questions
- Rule storage path proposed: `config/mixer/*.json` (chosen to be versionable and human-editable). Confirm if another location is preferred.
- Scope: this work item covers the core runtime (mix groups, priority/rule engine, voice-limiting, deterministic evaluation). It intentionally excludes CLI/demo scripting and visual intensity control unless requested.

Draft created for review. Please review and either (A) approve, or (B) provide edits/answers to the short clarifying questions below.

Clarifying questions (pick or freeform)
1) Confirm the rules file path: `config/mixer/*.json`? (Yes / Different path)
2) Confirm mix group list: footsteps, UI, combat, ambience, dialogue, alerts? (Yes / Add/Remove)
3) Do you want me to add Demo 9 (Runtime & State) as a blocking dependency on the work item now, or should we leave dependency recording to a Producer? (Add now / Leave to Producer)

Risks & assumptions
- Risk: Scope creep — implementing cross‑modal visuals, CLI demos, or Intelligence auto‑apply could expand work beyond core runtime. Mitigation: record additional features as separate work items and keep this item focused on core runtime implementation.
- Risk: Runtime/State interfaces change during Demo 9 work, causing rework. Mitigation: lock an interface contract (API surface) in a short integration spike or use feature toggles; coordinate with Demo 9 owners.
- Risk: Real‑time performance targets are missed under heavy load. Mitigation: add microbenchmarks to CI and measure worst‑case latency; implement graceful degradation (looser voice limits) as a fallback.
- Assumption: Rules will be authored as declarative JSON and are sufficient to express required duck/boost/limit logic; more complex decision logic will be suggested by Intelligence but only applied after human review.

Polish
- The proposed canonical rules path is `config/mixer/*.json` (versionable and human-editable). Example rule schema and a small set of example rules should be added in the first implementation PR.

Final draft: please confirm or provide last edits. Once approved I will remove this temporary draft file and keep the work item updated.
data.title: Demo 10: Mixer -- Intelligent Audio Balancing

data.description:
## Summary

Demonstrate the reasoning layer that synthesizes analysis, classification, and library data to provide actionable recommendations. Intelligence is assistive -- it suggests, the human decides.

## User Story

As a developer managing a sound library, I want an intelligent assistant that audits my library for gaps and redundancy, recommends sounds for specific use cases, and suggests exploration targets so I can build a complete, high-quality sound collection efficiently.

## What the User Sees

```bash
toneforge intelligence audit --library ./library
toneforge intelligence recommend --use-case "sci-fi menu navigation" --max-results 5
toneforge intelligence suggest-exploration --recipe footstep-stone
```

## New CLI Commands

- `toneforge intelligence audit --library <dir>`
- `toneforge intelligence recommend --use-case <desc> --max-results <n>`
- `toneforge intelligence suggest-exploration --recipe <r>`

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Audit identifies coverage gaps, redundancy, and quality issues
2. Recommendations include confidence scores and explanations
3. Exploration suggestions reference specific recipes and seed ranges
4. No Intelligence action modifies library data without human approval
5. All suggestions reference actionable CLI commands

## Reference PRDs

- `INTELLIGENCE_PRD.md`
- `docs/prd/DEMO_ROADMAP.md` (Demo 11)

## Dependencies

- Demo 7: Library (prerequisite)
- Demo 10: Mixer (prerequisite)
data.title: Demo 11: Intelligence -- Assisted Decision-Making

data.description:
## Summary

Transform curated library content into platform-specific, production-ready artifacts. The Validator ensures quality standards before compilation. The Compiler decides what stays procedural vs. what gets baked to WAV, and packages everything for target platforms.

## User Story

As a developer preparing for release, I want to validate my sound library against platform-specific quality rules and compile it into optimized, production-ready packages with deterministic build manifests.

## What the User Sees

```bash
toneforge validate --library ./library --ruleset mobile --strictness warning
toneforge compile --library ./library --category weapon --target web --rules presets/compile/web_defaults.json --output ./dist/web/
toneforge compile --library ./library --target mobile --rules presets/compile/mobile_aggressive.json --output ./dist/mobile/
toneforge compile --library ./library --target web --dry-run
```

## New CLI Commands

- `toneforge validate --library <dir> --ruleset <name> --strictness <level>`
- `toneforge compile --library <dir> --category <c> --target <platform> --rules <file> --output <dir>`
- `toneforge compile --dry-run`

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Validator checks: peak clipping, duration bounds, silence ratio
2. Validator produces structured JSON report with pass/warning/error counts
3. Compiler produces WAV files + manifest.json for target platform
4. Dry-run shows decisions without writing files
5. Build hashes enable deterministic build verification

## Reference PRDs

- `COMPILER_PRD.md`, `VALIDATOR_PRD.md`
- `docs/prd/DEMO_ROADMAP.md` (Demo 12)

## Dependencies

- Demo 7: Library (prerequisite)
- Demo 11: Intelligence (prerequisite)
data.title: Demo 12: Compiler & Validator -- Production-Ready Assets

data.description:
## Summary

Extend ToneForge beyond audio. The Visualizer generates deterministic, audio-synchronized visual effects. Haptics produces tactile feedback patterns. Palettes coordinate the aesthetic across all outputs.

## User Story

As a game developer, I want ToneForge to generate synchronized visual effects and haptic feedback patterns alongside audio so that all sensory outputs are perceptually aligned and aesthetically coordinated through palettes.

## What the User Sees

Web demo with visual canvas:
```
> palette.set("sci_fi_neon")
> runtime.play("weapon-laser-zap", { seed: 42, visual: true, haptic: true })
  [Audio: laser zap plays]
  [Visual: cyan directional streak, 150ms]
  [Haptic: sharp pulse, 50ms, intensity=0.8]
> palette.set("organic_forest")
> runtime.play("footstep-stone", { seed: 42, visual: true, haptic: true })
  [Visual: subtle ground ripple, earth tones]
  [Haptic: soft thump, 30ms, intensity=0.4]
```

```bash
toneforge visualize export --recipe weapon-laser-zap --seed 42 --format spritesheet --frames 8 --output ./vfx/
```

## New CLI Commands

- `toneforge visualize export --recipe <r> --seed <n> --format <f> --output <dir>`

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Visualizer produces animated effects synchronized to audio events
2. Haptic patterns match audio intensity and timing
3. Palette changes visually and perceptually alter all outputs
4. Sprite sheet export produces usable game assets
5. All outputs are deterministic for the same seed and palette

## Reference PRDs

- `VISUALIZE_PRD.md`, `HAPTICS_PRD.md`, `PALLETE_PRD.md`
- `docs/prd/DEMO_ROADMAP.md` (Demo 13)

## Dependencies

- Demo 9: Runtime & State (prerequisite)
- Demo 10: Mixer (prerequisite)
data.title: Demo 13: Visualizer & Haptics -- Cross-Modal Output

data.description:
## Summary

Demonstrate structured human goal representation and long-term experiential recall. Intent translates high-level directives ("make combat punchier") into actionable Intelligence queries. Memory tracks how assets are used, evaluated, and evolved over time.

## Prerequisite Demos

- Demo 11: Intelligence -- Assisted Decision-Making (TF-0MLXF98WZ1LQGM5W)

## New Modules

Intent, Memory

## What the User Sees

- Submit a structured intent: `toneforge intent submit --goal "reduce repetition in footstep sounds" --scope footsteps`
- Intelligence analysis produces actionable suggestions with human approval gate
- Query memory for historical context: `toneforge memory query --scope "weapon sounds" --time-range "last 30 days"`
- Memory informs intelligence recommendations via `--use-memory` flag

## New CLI Commands

- `toneforge intent submit --goal <desc> --scope <scope>`
- `toneforge memory query --scope <desc> --time-range <range>`
- `--use-memory` flag on intelligence commands

## Key Proof Points

- High-level goals translate into concrete, executable actions
- Memory prevents repeating mistakes and surfaces patterns humans miss
- Intent -> Intelligence pipeline maintains human approval at every step
- Historical data improves recommendation quality over time

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Intent submission produces an Intelligence analysis with actionable suggestions
2. No action executes without human approval
3. Memory records generation, promotion, and rejection events
4. Memory queries return time-scoped, scope-filtered reports
5. Intelligence recommendations improve when memory data is available

## Reference PRDs

INTENT_PRD.md, MEMORY_PRD.md
data.title: Demo 14: Intent & Memory -- Human-Centered Guidance

data.description:
## Summary

Demonstrate ToneForge operating across multiple clients in sync and embedded within external tools. Network replicates behavioral events (not audio streams) for deterministic multi-client playback. Integrations show ToneForge driving sound in a CI pipeline and exporting to game engine formats.

## Prerequisite Demos

- Demo 9: Runtime & State -- Real-Time Behavioral Sound (TF-0MLXF8TBT0DJDCO1)
- Demo 12: Compiler & Validator -- Production-Ready Assets (TF-0MLXF9FWG18T2ZHD)

## New Modules

Network, Integrations

## What the User Sees

**Network (two browser windows side by side):**
- Host broadcasts state changes to connected peers
- Client receives state changes and plays deterministically identical audio
- State transitions are synchronized across all connected clients

**Integrations (CI pipeline):**
- Generate, validate, compile, and export sounds in automated pipelines
- Export for Unity/web with platform-appropriate file structure

## New CLI Commands

- `toneforge sync --target <engine> --library <path> --output <path>`
- Network API: `network.host()`, `network.join()`

## Key Proof Points

- Network sync uses behavioral events (~50 bytes each), not audio streams (~1 MB/s)
- Determinism guarantees identical playback on all clients from the same events
- CI integration enables automated sound generation, validation, and packaging
- Engine adapters map ToneForge categories to platform-specific audio groups

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Two clients play the same sound sequence from shared behavioral events
2. Network bandwidth is under 1 KB/s for typical gameplay
3. CI pipeline validates, compiles, and exports without manual intervention
4. Unity/web export produces platform-appropriate file structure
5. Late-join clients receive state snapshot and sync correctly

## Reference PRDs

NETWORK_PRD.md, INTEGRATIONS_PRD.md
data.title: Demo 15: Network & Integrations -- Distributed & Embedded

data.description:
## Summary

Demonstrate publishing and consuming ToneForge assets through a marketplace. Assets are procedural systems (recipes, stacks, sequences, palettes), not static audio files -- buyers get infinite variation from a single purchase.

## Prerequisite Demos

- Demo 12: Compiler & Validator -- Production-Ready Assets (TF-0MLXF9FWG18T2ZHD)
- Demo 15: Network & Integrations -- Distributed & Embedded (TF-0MLXFBHHW1BBO1ZJ)

## New Modules

Marketplace

## What the User Sees

- Browse marketplace listings: `toneforge marketplace search --category "sci-fi weapons"`
- Install packages: `toneforge marketplace install industrial_lasers@2.1.0`
- Use installed recipes immediately with local registry
- Publish own packages with validation: `toneforge marketplace publish --package ./my-recipes/ --name "organic_footsteps" --version 1.0.0`

## New CLI Commands

- `toneforge marketplace search --category <c>`
- `toneforge marketplace install <package>@<version>`
- `toneforge marketplace publish --package <dir> --name <n> --version <v>`

## Key Proof Points

- Marketplace assets are procedural systems, not static files -- infinite variation from a single package
- Validator gates publishing -- no broken or invalid assets reach the marketplace
- Semantic versioning ensures compatibility
- Installed recipes integrate seamlessly with the local registry

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Search returns relevant packages with metadata (recipes, stacks, ratings, license)
2. Install adds recipes to the local registry and they work immediately
3. Publish validates the package before uploading
4. Installed packages maintain determinism (same seed = same output)
5. Version conflicts are detected and reported

## Reference PRDs

MARKETPLACE_PRD.md

data.description:
## Summary

Author `demos/mvp-1.md` by extracting narrative content from the bash script and TypeScript data file, and document the markdown convention in `demos/README.md`.

## Player Experience Change

No direct user-visible change. This feature creates the source-of-truth content file that all subsequent features consume. Demo authors and reviewers can now read and edit demo content in standard markdown.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. `demos/mvp-1.md` contains all narrative content from `scripts/demo/mvp_1.sh` and `web/src/demo-content.ts` (intro, 4 acts, recap)
2. YAML front matter includes: `title`, `id`, `description` fields
3. H2 headings delineate steps; blockquotes contain problem statements; paragraphs contain solutions; fenced code blocks contain executable commands; `> [!commentary]` admonitions contain post-command commentary
4. `demos/README.md` documents the markdown convention (heading structure, code block semantics, admonition syntax, front matter schema)
5. Markdown renders correctly in GitHub web preview (no broken formatting, all code blocks display, admonitions show as blockquotes)
6. No executable code or ANSI escape sequences appear in the markdown content

## Minimal Implementation

- Create `demos/` directory
- Extract and convert all narrative from `mvp_1.sh` and `demo-content.ts` into `demos/mvp-1.md`
- Write `demos/README.md` convention spec
- Validate rendered markdown in GitHub preview

## Dependencies

None (first feature in the chain)

## Deliverables

- `demos/mvp-1.md`
- `demos/README.md`
data.title: Demo Markdown Content & Convention

data.description:
## Summary

Implement a platform-independent parser (`src/demo/parser.ts`) that reads demo markdown and produces a typed step array using `gray-matter` + `remark` (unified/mdast ecosystem).

## Player Experience Change

No direct user-visible change. This module provides the shared parsing layer consumed by both the CLI runner and web adapter. Developers get a typed, tested API for converting demo markdown into structured step data.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. `parseDemoMarkdown(rawMarkdown: string)` returns a typed array of demo steps with fields for: id, label, title, problem, solution, description, commands, commentary
2. Uses `gray-matter` for front matter extraction and `remark` (unified) for AST parsing
3. Parser extracts commentary text from `> [!commentary]` blocks and assigns it to the `commentary` field of the corresponding step
4. No Node.js-specific or browser-specific APIs (works in both environments)
5. Golden snapshot test: parsing `demos/mvp-1.md` produces expected output matching current `DEMO_STEPS` content
6. Edge case tests: empty code blocks, missing front matter, steps without commands, malformed admonitions
7. Parser returns an error or empty array for a completely empty markdown string
8. Parser handles steps with no commands (intro/recap) by returning an empty commands array

## Prototype / Experiment

Validate that `remark` can parse `> [!commentary]` admonitions in both Node.js and browser environments. Success threshold: `parseDemoMarkdown` returns the correct commentary text for all 4 acts of mvp-1.md when run via `npx tsx` (Node) and via Vite dev server (browser). If `remark` does not natively handle admonitions, implement a custom `remark` plugin and verify it works in both environments.

## Minimal Implementation

- Add `gray-matter`, `remark`, `remark-parse`, and `unified` dependencies
- Implement `src/demo/parser.ts` with typed output interface
- Implement `src/demo/parser.test.ts` with golden snapshot + edge cases
- Export types: `DemoMeta`, `ParsedDemoStep`, `parseDemoMarkdown`

## Dependencies

- Feature 1: Demo Markdown Content & Convention (TF-0MLXH2LCP0R2CA97) -- demo content must exist to test against

## Deliverables

- `src/demo/parser.ts`
- `src/demo/parser.test.ts`
- Updated `package.json`
data.title: Markdown Demo Parser

data.description:
## Summary

Implement a TypeScript CLI runner (`scripts/demo/run.ts`) that replaces `mvp_1.sh`, reading parsed markdown and presenting the same interactive terminal experience with ANSI formatting, pauses, and live command execution.

## Player Experience Change

Team members run `npx tsx scripts/demo/run.ts` instead of `./scripts/demo/mvp_1.sh`. The experience is visually equivalent: banner, problem/solution blocks, live command execution with output, commentary, and pause prompts. Additionally, users can select from multiple demos via an interactive list picker or `--demo` flag.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. `npx tsx scripts/demo/run.ts` runs the demo with ANSI formatting, pauses, and live command execution
2. Output includes: banner with title, PROBLEM/SOLUTION blocks for each act, command execution with `$` prefix, commentary in dim text, and pause prompts between sections
3. Supports `--demo <name>` flag to select a demo file from `demos/`
4. Interactive list picker appears when no `--demo` flag is provided and multiple demos exist
5. Lists available demos with `--list` flag
6. Preflight checks: verifies dependencies installed and build exists
7. Running with `--demo nonexistent` prints an error and exits with code 1
8. No bash wrapper -- pure TypeScript

## Minimal Implementation

- Implement `scripts/demo/run.ts` consuming `parseDemoMarkdown`
- ANSI formatting helpers (bold, dim, cyan, green, yellow, magenta)
- Interactive stdin-based pause (`Press Enter to continue...`)
- Shell command execution via `child_process.execSync` or `spawn`
- Demo discovery: glob `demos/*.md`, parse front matter for titles
- Add npm script: `"demo": "tsx scripts/demo/run.ts"`

## Dependencies

- Feature 2: Markdown Demo Parser (TF-0MLXH2ZTZ0J2RLFZ) -- parser must exist to consume markdown

## Deliverables

- `scripts/demo/run.ts`
- Updated `package.json` scripts
data.title: CLI Demo Runner

data.description:
## Summary

Refactor `web/src/demo-content.ts` to import markdown via Vite `?raw`, parse it with the shared parser, and update the wizard to consume the parser output shape. Add demo selection dropdown.

## Player Experience Change

The web demo wizard displays the same stepped walkthrough experience as before, but content is now sourced from markdown files. Users see a demo selection dropdown when multiple demos exist. Audio playback via Tone.js continues to work. The lazy-loading pattern for Tone.js is preserved.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. `web/src/demo-content.ts` imports markdown from `demos/` via Vite `?raw` and re-exports parsed steps
2. Vite config has a resolve alias: `@demos` pointing to repo-root `demos/` directory
3. Wizard (`web/src/wizard.ts`) renders parsed demo steps with the same visual experience (problem/solution blocks, commands, Run button, commentary)
4. Demo selection dropdown lists all .md files from `demos/` with their front matter titles; selecting a demo reloads the wizard steps
5. Audio handler (`web/src/audio.ts`) continues to extract command strings and play audio for generate commands
6. Web dev server (`npm run dev`) and production build (`npm run build`) both work
7. No hardcoded demo content remains in TypeScript
8. If markdown import fails (missing file), the web app shows an error message rather than a blank page

## Prototype / Experiment

Validate that Vite `?raw` imports work with a resolve alias pointing to `demos/` outside `web/src/`. Success threshold: `import content from '@demos/mvp-1.md?raw'` resolves correctly in both dev server and production build. If it fails, fall back to symlink approach.

## Minimal Implementation

- Update `web/vite.config.ts` with resolve alias: `'@demos': path.resolve(__dirname, '../demos')`
- Refactor `web/src/demo-content.ts`: import `mvp-1.md?raw`, parse with `parseDemoMarkdown`, export steps
- Update `web/src/wizard.ts` to use parser output types instead of old `DemoStep`
- Add demo selector dropdown to wizard nav
- Verify `web/src/audio.ts` command parsing still works
- Update `web/src/main.ts` if needed for demo selection routing

## Dependencies

- Feature 1: Demo Markdown Content & Convention (TF-0MLXH2LCP0R2CA97) -- markdown content must exist
- Feature 2: Markdown Demo Parser (TF-0MLXH2ZTZ0J2RLFZ) -- parser must exist to parse markdown

## Deliverables

- Updated `web/vite.config.ts`
- Updated `web/src/demo-content.ts`
- Updated `web/src/wizard.ts`
- Updated `web/src/main.ts` (if needed)
data.title: Web Adapter & Wizard Integration

## Summary

Update all existing tests to work with the new markdown-driven content source, including unit tests, integration tests, and Playwright E2E tests.

## Player Experience Change

No direct user-visible change. This feature ensures the refactored demo engine passes all quality gates, validating that the CLI and web experiences are functionally equivalent to the pre-refactoring versions.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. `web/test/commands.test.ts` imports demo steps from the new parser-based content source and all assertions pass
2. Playwright E2E tests navigate the wizard, click Run buttons, and verify audio playback -- all using the new content source
3. `npx vitest run` passes in both root and web projects with zero failures
4. Playwright E2E tests pass (`npx playwright test`)
5. Zero test files import directly from old hardcoded `demo-content.ts` content (all imports go through the parser-based adapter)
6. Parser integration test exists: parse `demos/mvp-1.md` and validate each step has valid commands referencing registered recipes

## Minimal Implementation

- Update `web/test/commands.test.ts` import path and step shape assertions
- Update Playwright E2E test selectors/assertions if the wizard DOM structure changed
- Add parser integration test: parse `mvp-1.md`, validate each step command references a registered recipe
- Run full test suite (root + web + E2E) and fix any breakage

## Dependencies

- Feature 3: CLI Demo Runner (TF-0MLXH3BX91X31AWK) -- CLI runner must exist
- Feature 4: Web Adapter & Wizard Integration (TF-0MLXH3S7H0H1UA4H) -- web adapter must be complete

## Deliverables

- Updated `web/test/commands.test.ts`
- Updated Playwright E2E tests
- New parser integration test

data.title: Cleanup & Documentation

data.description:
## Summary

The intro and recap steps in both the CLI demo runner and web wizard display their text content as a wall of text with no visual separation between paragraphs and lists. The act steps (which use problem/solution/commands/commentary) look great.

## Root Cause

The parser's nodesToDescription function joins paragraphs with single newlines, collapsing distinct paragraphs into a continuous block. Both renderers then display this flat text without structural formatting:
- CLI: printDescription prints line-by-line with no blank line between paragraphs
- Web: puts the entire description into a `<pre>` tag, showing it as monospace with no paragraph breaks

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Parser separates paragraphs with double newlines (\n\n) in the description field
2. CLI runner renders description text with proper blank lines between paragraphs and visual distinction for list items
3. Web wizard renders description as structured HTML (paragraphs and ordered/unordered lists) instead of a `<pre>` block
4. All existing tests pass (root 98, web 35, E2E 5)
5. Build succeeds
data.title: Improve intro and recap text formatting in CLI and web demo

data.description:
## Summary

Create `bin/dev-cli.js` that dynamically registers tsx and imports `src/cli.ts`, and update `package.json` `bin` field to register both `tf` and `toneforge`.

## Context

Part of Install the CLI (TF-0MLW2K2V51AY2N9T). No `bin/` directory exists. The current `package.json` maps `"toneforge": "dist/cli.js"`. This feature creates the dev-mode loader that allows running the CLI from TypeScript source without a build step.

### User Experience Change

Developers can run `./bin/dev-cli.js generate --recipe ui-scifi-confirm --seed 42` directly, getting the same result as `npx tsx src/cli.ts generate ...` but with a shorter, more conventional path. Code changes in `src/` are reflected immediately without rebuilding.

### Reference Documents

- `docs/prd/CLI_PRD.md` -- CLI command model
- TF-0MLW3L9SN041842J -- CLI startup performance (tsx overhead findings)
- `src/cli.ts` -- CLI entry point that the loader imports

## Acceptance Criteria

1. `bin/dev-cli.js` exists with `#!/usr/bin/env node` shebang, dynamically imports tsx to register TypeScript support, then imports `src/cli.ts` and calls `main()`
2. `package.json` `bin` field maps both `tf` and `toneforge` to `bin/dev-cli.js`
3. `./bin/dev-cli.js generate --recipe ui-scifi-confirm --seed 42` produces the same output as `npx tsx src/cli.ts generate --recipe ui-scifi-confirm --seed 42`
4. After editing a console.log string in `src/cli.ts`, running `./bin/dev-cli.js --help` reflects the change without running `npm run build`
5. `npm run build && node dist/cli.js --help` continues to work unchanged
6. `bin/dev-cli.js` has the executable permission bit set
7. Startup time for `tf generate --recipe ui-scifi-confirm --seed 42` is under 5 seconds

## Minimal Implementation

- Create `bin/dev-cli.js` with shebang, tsx registration via `await import('tsx/esm/api')`, then `const { main } = await import('../src/cli.ts'); process.exitCode = await main();`
- Update `package.json` `bin` to `{ "tf": "bin/dev-cli.js", "toneforge": "bin/dev-cli.js" }`
- Verify tsx pinned in devDependencies (already `^4.21.0`)

## Dependencies

None (first feature in the chain)

## Deliverables

- `bin/dev-cli.js`
- Updated `package.json`
data.title: Dev Loader Script & Dual Bin Entries

data.description:
## Summary

Add a postinstall lifecycle script that runs `npm link` so `tf` and `toneforge` are available on PATH after `npm install`.

## Context

Part of Install the CLI (TF-0MLW2K2V51AY2N9T). After Feature 1 creates the dev loader and bin entries, this feature ensures they are symlinked onto the developer's PATH automatically. The postinstall hook handles this transparently.

### User Experience Change

After running `npm install`, developers can immediately type `tf generate --recipe ui-scifi-confirm --seed 42` without any additional setup. The `toneforge` alias also works. No manual `npm link` step required.

### Reference Documents

- TF-0MLW2K2V51AY2N9T -- parent work item (risk section on npm link fragility)
- npm docs on lifecycle scripts

## Acceptance Criteria

1. After `npm install`, `tf --help` prints ToneForge help text
2. After `npm install`, `toneforge --help` prints the same help text
3. If `npm link` fails (e.g., restricted global installs in CI), the error is caught and a warning is logged, but `npm install` completes successfully
4. Running `npm install` twice produces no errors or warnings from the link step
5. npm scripts `start`, `generate`, and `demo` continue to work
6. Running `npm install` in a fresh clone as a non-root user succeeds and `tf --help` works

## Minimal Implementation

- Add `"postinstall": "npm link || echo 'Warning: npm link failed. Run tf via ./bin/dev-cli.js instead.'"` to `package.json` scripts
- Test on a clean clone: `git clone && npm install && tf generate --recipe ui-scifi-confirm --seed 42`

## Dependencies

- Feature 1: Dev Loader Script & Dual Bin Entries (TF-0MLXLVXVQ17MX7XN)

## Deliverables

- Updated `package.json` scripts section
data.title: Automated npm link via Lifecycle Hook

data.description:
## Summary

Update all `npx tsx src/cli.ts` and `node dist/cli.js` references in docs, demos, tests, and scripts to use `tf`.

## Context

Part of Install the CLI (TF-0MLW2K2V51AY2N9T). After Features 1 and 2 make `tf` available on PATH, all existing documentation, demo scripts, test fixtures, and runner scripts need updating to use the new short command form.

### User Experience Change

Developers reading README, demo markdown, or test code see `tf generate ...` as the canonical invocation. The old `npx tsx src/cli.ts` and `node dist/cli.js` forms are eliminated from all non-generated files.

### Reference Documents

- TF-0MLW2K2V51AY2N9T -- parent work item (related files section)
- `README.md` line 33 -- current `npx tsx src/cli.ts` invocation
- `demos/mvp-1.md` -- 5 occurrences of `node dist/cli.js`
- `demos/README.md` -- 1 occurrence
- `scripts/demo/run.ts` line 199 -- preflight check for `dist/cli.js`
- `web/test/audio.test.ts` -- 6 test fixture strings with `node dist/cli.js`
- `web/e2e/tutorial.spec.ts` line 173 -- comment with `node dist/cli.js`
- `web/src/audio.ts` -- `extractSeed()` and `isGenerateCommand()` parsing functions

## Acceptance Criteria

1. `README.md` uses `tf generate ...` instead of `npx tsx src/cli.ts generate ...`
2. `demos/mvp-1.md` uses `tf generate ...` instead of `node dist/cli.js generate ...` (all 5 occurrences)
3. `demos/README.md` uses `tf generate ...` instead of `node dist/cli.js generate ...`
4. `scripts/demo/run.ts` preflight checks for `tf` availability or `bin/dev-cli.js` existence instead of `dist/cli.js`
5. `web/test/audio.test.ts` test fixtures use `tf generate ...`; `extractSeed()` and `isGenerateCommand()` updated to parse the new format only
6. `web/e2e/tutorial.spec.ts` comment updated to use `tf` invocation
7. `grep -rn "npx tsx src/cli" . --exclude-dir={node_modules,dist,.git} --exclude=package-lock.json` returns zero matches
8. `grep -rn "node dist/cli.js" . --exclude-dir={node_modules,dist,.git} --exclude=package-lock.json` returns zero matches
9. All existing tests pass (`npm test` and `npm run test:e2e`)
10. `npm run demo` completes all steps without invocation-related errors

## Minimal Implementation

- Search-and-replace across all invocation sites identified in seed context
- Inspect `web/src/audio.ts` to verify `extractSeed()` / `isGenerateCommand()` use regex patterns and update to match `tf generate ...` format
- Update `scripts/demo/run.ts` `preflight()` to check `bin/dev-cli.js` or `tf` on PATH instead of `dist/cli.js`
- Run full test suite and demo runner to verify

## Dependencies

- Feature 2: Automated npm link via Lifecycle Hook (TF-0MLXLW9FX1JT0145)

## Deliverables

- Updated files: `README.md`, `demos/mvp-1.md`, `demos/README.md`, `scripts/demo/run.ts`, `web/src/audio.ts`, `web/test/audio.test.ts`, `web/e2e/tutorial.spec.ts`
data.title: Migrate All Invocation References

data.description:
## Summary

Refactor the offline renderer to discover recipes via the RecipeRegistry instead of hardcoded `SUPPORTED_RECIPES` array and per-recipe switch statements, extending the recipe registration to include `getParams`, `getDuration`, and `buildOfflineGraph` capabilities.

## Context

Part of Demo 1: Recipe Variety (TF-0MLXF74KE17SQKAD). The current renderer (`src/core/renderer.ts`) maintains a hardcoded `SUPPORTED_RECIPES` array and uses per-recipe switch statements in `buildRecipeGraph()` and `getRecipeDuration()`. This makes adding new recipes cumbersome — every new recipe requires modifying the renderer. This feature refactors the renderer to use the RecipeRegistry for discovery, and extends the registration entry to include params, duration, and offline graph builder capabilities.

### User Experience Change

No direct user-facing change. After this feature, adding a new recipe no longer requires modifying `renderer.ts` or `cli.ts` — only registering in `src/recipes/index.ts`. This unblocks all subsequent recipe features.

### Reference Documents

- `docs/prd/CORE_PRD.md` Section 4.1 (Recipe interface), Section 8 (offline rendering)
- `src/core/renderer.ts` — current hardcoded renderer
- `src/core/recipe.ts` — RecipeRegistry class
- `src/recipes/index.ts` — recipe registration

## Acceptance Criteria

1. `SUPPORTED_RECIPES` constant and `buildRecipeGraph`/`getRecipeDuration` switch statements are removed from `renderer.ts`
2. The renderer resolves recipe name to an offline graph builder via the RecipeRegistry
3. Adding a new recipe requires only: (a) creating param + factory files, (b) registering in `src/recipes/index.ts`. No changes to `renderer.ts` or `cli.ts` are needed
4. Existing `ui-scifi-confirm` renderer determinism tests (10x byte-identical) continue to pass unchanged
5. The Recipe registration entry includes `getParams(rng)`, `getDuration(rng)`, and `buildOfflineGraph(rng, ctx, duration)` callables
6. `ui-scifi-confirm` is migrated to the new registration shape
7. `cli.ts` uses the registry for recipe validation instead of `SUPPORTED_RECIPES`

## Minimal Implementation

- Extend the `RecipeRegistry.register()` signature to accept params/duration/offlineBuilder alongside the Tone.js factory
- Migrate `ui-scifi-confirm` registration in `src/recipes/index.ts` to the new shape
- Refactor `renderRecipe()` in `renderer.ts` to look up the recipe from the registry and call its offline builder
- Remove `SUPPORTED_RECIPES`, `buildRecipeGraph`, `getRecipeDuration`
- Update `src/cli.ts` to use the registry for recipe validation
- Run all existing tests to confirm no regressions

## Dependencies

None (first feature in the chain)

## Deliverables

- Updated `src/core/renderer.ts`
- Updated `src/core/recipe.ts`
- Updated `src/recipes/index.ts`
- Updated `src/cli.ts`
- All existing tests green
data.title: Renderer Refactor — Registry-Based Discovery

data.description:
## Summary

Create a reusable test helper that runs the standard battery of recipe tests (parameter variation, determinism, range bounds, registry integration, renderer determinism) against any recipe name, reducing boilerplate for the 4 new recipes.

## Context

Part of Demo 1: Recipe Variety (TF-0MLXF74KE17SQKAD). The existing `ui-scifi-confirm.test.ts` contains a well-structured test pattern that should be replicated for all new recipes. Rather than copying the full test file 4 times, this feature extracts a shared helper that runs the standard battery and supports recipe-specific extensions.

### User Experience Change

No direct user-facing change. Reduces test boilerplate and ensures consistent test coverage across all recipes.

### Reference Documents

- `src/recipes/ui-scifi-confirm.test.ts` — reference test pattern
- `src/core/renderer.test.ts` — renderer determinism test pattern

## Acceptance Criteria

1. A shared test utility function accepts a recipe name and parameter specifications and runs: variation across 5 seeds, determinism (same seed 2x), range bounds (50 seeds), registry integration
2. The helper also supports renderer determinism tests (10x byte-identical renders)
3. `ui-scifi-confirm.test.ts` is refactored to use the shared helper with existing assertions preserved
4. All existing recipe and renderer tests continue to pass
5. Helper supports recipe-specific additional tests via callback parameter

## Minimal Implementation

- Create `src/test-utils/recipe-test-helper.ts` with a `describeRecipe(name, paramSpec, extraTests?)` function
- Define `ParamSpec` type: `{ name: string, min: number, max: number }[]`
- Implement standard test battery: variation, determinism, range bounds, registry, renderer determinism
- Refactor `ui-scifi-confirm.test.ts` to use the helper
- Run tests to confirm no regressions

## Dependencies

- Feature 1: Renderer Refactor (TF-0MLYAL9D413ZV71P)

## Deliverables

- `src/test-utils/recipe-test-helper.ts`
- Updated `src/recipes/ui-scifi-confirm.test.ts`
- All existing tests green
data.title: Shared Recipe Test Helper

data.description:
## Summary

Implement the `weapon-laser-zap` recipe — a short, punchy laser/blaster sound using FM synthesis + noise burst, following the two-file pattern (params module + Tone.js factory + offline graph builder).

## Context

Part of Demo 1: Recipe Variety (TF-0MLXF74KE17SQKAD). This is one of 4 new recipes demonstrating that ToneForge's recipe architecture generalizes across game audio categories. The weapon category is typically well-suited to pure synthesis, making this a lower-risk recipe.

### User Experience Change

Developers can run `toneforge generate --recipe weapon-laser-zap --seed 42` to hear a procedurally generated laser/blaster sound. Different seeds produce distinct variations in pitch, modulation depth, and noise burst character.

### Reference Documents

- `docs/prd/DEMO_ROADMAP.md` line 56 — weapon-laser-zap synthesis approach
- `docs/prd/CORE_PRD.md` Section 11 — weapon recipe ideas
- `src/recipes/ui-scifi-confirm-params.ts` — reference params module pattern
- `src/recipes/ui-scifi-confirm.ts` — reference Tone.js factory pattern

## Acceptance Criteria

1. `weapon-laser-zap` is registered in the RecipeRegistry and discoverable via `registry.list()`
2. Produces audibly distinct output across 5+ different seeds; at least 3 seed-varied parameters differ across 5 seeds (verified by unit test)
3. Passes determinism tests (10x byte-identical renders for the same seed)
4. Params module has zero audio dependencies; exports typed params with range-bounded values
5. Synthesis uses FM synthesis + noise burst per the DEMO_ROADMAP spec
6. Duration is natural for the category (short, punchy — roughly 0.05-0.3s)

## Minimal Implementation

- Create `src/recipes/weapon-laser-zap-params.ts` with seed-derived FM params: carrier frequency (200-2000 Hz), modulator frequency (50-500 Hz), modulation index (1-10), noise burst level (0.1-0.5), attack (0.001-0.005s), decay (0.03-0.25s)
- Create `src/recipes/weapon-laser-zap.ts` with Tone.js factory using FM oscillator + noise source + bandpass filter + amplitude envelope
- Register in `src/recipes/index.ts` with offline graph builder
- Add tests using the shared test helper + recipe-specific FM synthesis assertions

## Dependencies

- Feature 1: Renderer Refactor (TF-0MLYAL9D413ZV71P)
- Feature 2: Shared Recipe Test Helper (TF-0MLYALM73126AX8A)

## Deliverables

- `src/recipes/weapon-laser-zap-params.ts`
- `src/recipes/weapon-laser-zap.ts`
- `src/recipes/weapon-laser-zap.test.ts`
- Updated `src/recipes/index.ts`
data.title: Weapon Laser Zap Recipe

data.description:
## Summary

Implement the `footstep-stone` recipe — a procedural footstep on hard surface using filtered noise + transient shaping, following the two-file pattern.

## Context

Part of Demo 1: Recipe Variety (TF-0MLXF74KE17SQKAD). This recipe demonstrates that the procedural recipe pattern works for impact/transient sounds. The epic notes that footstep sounds are harder to synthesize convincingly with pure synthesis than UI sounds; the goal is architectural validation, not sonic perfection.

### User Experience Change

Developers can run `toneforge generate --recipe footstep-stone --seed 42` to hear a procedurally generated footstep sound on a hard surface. Different seeds produce distinct variations in transient character, resonance, and tonal quality.

### Reference Documents

- `docs/prd/DEMO_ROADMAP.md` line 57 — footstep-stone synthesis approach
- `docs/prd/CORE_PRD.md` Section 6.1 — footstep recipe sketch
- `src/recipes/ui-scifi-confirm-params.ts` — reference params module pattern

## Acceptance Criteria

1. `footstep-stone` is registered in the RecipeRegistry and discoverable via `registry.list()`
2. Produces audibly distinct output across 5+ different seeds; at least 3 seed-varied parameters differ across 5 seeds (verified by unit test)
3. Passes determinism tests (10x byte-identical renders for the same seed)
4. Params module has zero audio dependencies; exports typed params with range-bounded values
5. Synthesis uses filtered noise + transient shaping per the DEMO_ROADMAP spec
6. Duration is natural for the category (roughly 0.05-0.2s)
7. Sound produces a recognizable percussive transient; sonic perfection is not required — this is architectural validation

## Minimal Implementation

- Create `src/recipes/footstep-stone-params.ts` with seed-derived params: noise filter center frequency (200-800 Hz), bandwidth/Q (1-10), transient attack (0.001-0.005s), decay (0.02-0.15s), resonance frequency (100-500 Hz), resonance gain (0.1-0.5)
- Create `src/recipes/footstep-stone.ts` with Tone.js factory using noise source + bandpass filter + resonant lowpass + sharp amplitude envelope
- Register in `src/recipes/index.ts` with offline graph builder
- Add tests using the shared test helper

## Dependencies

- Feature 1: Renderer Refactor (TF-0MLYAL9D413ZV71P)
- Feature 2: Shared Recipe Test Helper (TF-0MLYALM73126AX8A)

## Deliverables

- `src/recipes/footstep-stone-params.ts`
- `src/recipes/footstep-stone.ts`
- `src/recipes/footstep-stone.test.ts`
- Updated `src/recipes/index.ts`
data.title: Footstep Stone Recipe
