## Summary

Add the `toneforge analyze` command to the CLI with `--input <file>` support for single WAV file analysis, including `--format json` (default) and `--json` flag.

## Player Experience Change

The user can now run `toneforge analyze --input <path.wav>` to get structured analysis metrics for any WAV file. This is the primary user-facing entry point for the analysis feature. JSON output shows all computed metrics (duration, peak, RMS, crest factor, attack time, spectral centroid, quality flags).

## Acceptance Criteria

- `toneforge analyze --input <path.wav>` analyzes a single WAV file and outputs structured JSON to stdout
- `--format json` is the default for single-file mode
- `--json` flag outputs structured JSON to stdout (existing convention)
- Without `--json`, outputs human-readable styled text with metric labels and values
- Help text (`toneforge analyze --help`) follows existing CLI patterns (markdown-rendered)
- Exit code 0 on success, 1 on error with error JSON on stderr
- Error handling: file not found, invalid WAV format, empty file
- Exit code 1 and error message when `--input` and `--recipe` are both provided (mutually exclusive)
- Integration test: generate a WAV, analyze it, verify JSON output contains all expected metric fields

## Minimal Implementation

- Add `analyze` command to CLI argument parser in `src/cli.ts`
- Wire up: parse `--input`, read WAV via decoder (Feature 6), create analysis engine with all extractors (Features 1-5), format output
- Add `showAnalyzeHelp()` function following existing pattern
- Add `analyze` to the main help text command list
- Integration test using `execFile`

## Dependencies

- Feature 1: Analysis Engine Core & Registry (TF-0MM03GUUH01Z5NHZ)
- Feature 2: Time-Domain Metrics Extractor (TF-0MM03H7F519XMJZ3)
- Feature 3: Quality Flags Extractor (TF-0MM03HH4G11PLP51)
- Feature 4: Attack Time Extractor (TF-0MM03HURO1CXXB3K)
- Feature 5: Spectral Centroid Extractor (TF-0MM03I8KJ1XC600O)
- Feature 6: WAV File Decoder (TF-0MM03IN391MK8G1E)

## Deliverables

- Updated `src/cli.ts`
- `src/cli.e2e.test.ts` additions (analyze command integration tests)

## Summary

Add `--recipe <name> --seed <n>` support to the analyze command, rendering internally and analyzing without writing to disk.

## Player Experience Change

The user can now run `toneforge analyze --recipe weapon-laser-zap --seed 42` to analyze a generated sound directly without first exporting it as a WAV file. This enables rapid iteration: generate and measure in a single command.

## Acceptance Criteria

- `toneforge analyze --recipe <name> --seed <n>` renders the recipe internally and outputs analysis
- Uses existing `renderRecipe()` from `src/core/renderer.ts`
- Error handling: unknown recipe name (exit code 1 with descriptive error), invalid seed value
- Output format follows same `--format` and `--json` conventions as file mode
- Integration test: analyze a known recipe+seed, verify deterministic output matches expected structure

## Minimal Implementation

- Add `--recipe` and `--seed` argument handling to the analyze command in `src/cli.ts`
- When `--recipe` is provided: call `renderRecipe(name, seed)`, pass `result.samples` and `result.sampleRate` to the analysis engine
- Reuse existing output formatting from Feature 7

## Dependencies

- Feature 7: CLI Analyze Command - Single File (TF-0MM03J2OH13ZZVOW)

## Deliverables

- Updated `src/cli.ts`
- Additional integration tests in `src/cli.e2e.test.ts`
data.title: CLI Analyze -- Recipe+Seed Mode

data.description:
## Summary

Add batch analysis (`--input <directory>`) with one JSON file per WAV, output directory support (`--output <dir>`), and `--format table` for human-readable summary.

## Player Experience Change

The user can now analyze an entire directory of WAV files in a single command and see results as a table or as individual JSON files. This enables batch quality control: generate 20 seed variants, analyze the batch, scan the table for outliers, and inspect flagged sounds individually.

## Acceptance Criteria

- `--input <directory>` discovers all `.wav` files in the directory and analyzes each
- `--output <dir>` writes one JSON file per input WAV to the specified directory
- When `--output` directory does not exist, creates it automatically (matching existing CLI behavior for `--output` in the generate command)
- `--format table` outputs a human-readable summary table with key metrics per file (filename, duration, peak, RMS, crest factor, spectral centroid, quality flags)
- Table uses existing `outputTable()` utility from `src/output.ts`
- Without `--output`, batch results are printed to stdout (JSON array or table depending on `--format`)
- Handles empty directories gracefully (prints message, exit code 0)
- Prints file count summary (e.g., "Analyzed 20 files")
- Integration test: generate batch WAVs with seed-range, analyze directory, verify output files exist and table format is correct

## Minimal Implementation

- Directory scanning: `readdir` + filter `.wav` files, sort alphabetically
- Loop over files: decode WAV, run analysis engine, write JSON to output dir
- Table formatter: column headers, aligned numeric values, quality flag indicators (e.g., "!clip", "!silent")
- File count progress/summary output
- Create output directory if it does not exist (`mkdir -p` equivalent)

## Dependencies

- Feature 7: CLI Analyze Command - Single File (TF-0MM03J2OH13ZZVOW)

## Deliverables

- Updated `src/cli.ts`
- Additional integration tests in `src/cli.e2e.test.ts`

## Player Experience Change

New users can follow a step-by-step walkthrough to learn the analysis feature. The demo explains what each metric means in plain language and shows real CLI output from actual commands.

## Acceptance Criteria

- File at `demos/audio-analysis.md` follows existing demo format (YAML front matter, multi-act structure, CLI commands with commentary)
- Demonstrates: single file analysis (JSON output), batch analysis (table output), recipe+seed analysis, quality flag inspection
- Shows real CLI output from actual commands (not fabricated)
- Explains what each metric means in plain language (duration, peak, RMS, crest factor, attack time, spectral centroid)
- Quality flags section explains how to interpret clipping and silence flags
- Can be followed step-by-step by a new user from a clean state

## Minimal Implementation

- Act 2: Batch analysis of a seed range (table output, scan for outliers)

## Summary

Move the act navigation tabs to the right of the demo dropdown selector so they sit on the same row, instead of being stacked vertically (dropdown on top, tabs below).

## Current State

In `web/src/wizard.ts`, the demo selector (`.wizard-demo-selector`, lines 32-61) and the step nav (`.wizard-nav`, lines 63-79) are appended as separate block-level children of the wizard container. In `web/src/index.html`, their CSS is at lines 93-124.

The dropdown is a flex row with label+select. The nav is a separate flex row with wrapped buttons. They stack vertically.

## Desired Change

Combine the demo selector and act tabs into a single flex row. The dropdown appears on the left, act tab buttons flow to its right. The border-bottom should remain on the combined row.

## Implementation Approach

Option A (CSS-only): Wrap both elements in a container div with `display: flex; align-items: center;` and remove the bottom margin from the selector.

Option B (TS refactor): In `wizard.ts`, create a single wrapper div that contains both the selector and nav elements. Style the wrapper as a flex row.

Either approach works. The key constraint is that both elements must be on the same row with the dropdown left-aligned and tabs flowing to its right.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Demo dropdown and act tab buttons appear on the same horizontal row
- Dropdown is left-aligned, tabs flow to its right
- Bottom border spans the full width of the combined row
- Existing click handlers for both dropdown and tab buttons continue to work
- Layout does not break with many act tabs (flex-wrap still applies)

## Files to Change

- `web/src/index.html` -- CSS for `.wizard-demo-selector` and `.wizard-nav`
- `web/src/wizard.ts` -- DOM structure for selector and nav (lines 32-79)

## Summary The metric explanations in Act 2 of `demos/audio-analysis.md` are formatted as paragraphs with bold labels (`**duration** (seconds) -- ...`) separated by blank lines. The wizard's list detection in `web/src/wizard.ts` (lines 169-171) checks if ALL lines in a block match list patterns (`/^\d+\.\s/` or `/^[-*]\s/`). Since the current format uses multi-paragraph bold-label blocks, the wizard renders them as plain `

` tags rather than a `

    `. ## Current State (lines 83-114 in demos/audio-analysis.md) ```markdown **duration** (seconds) -- How long the sound is. This laser zap is 0.108 seconds -- about 108 milliseconds, a short burst. **peak** (0.0 to 1.0+) -- The highest absolute sample value... ``` Each metric is a separate paragraph. The wizard sees them as individual text blocks and renders `

    ` for each. ## Desired Change Reformat the metric descriptions as a proper markdown unordered list so the wizard detects and renders them as `

    • ...
    `: ```markdown - **duration** (seconds) -- How long the sound is... - **peak** (0.0 to 1.0+) -- The highest absolute sample value... ``` Each metric becomes a list item starting with `- `. Multi-line items must NOT have blank lines between them (the wizard splits on double newlines to identify blocks, then checks if all lines in a block match list patterns). ## Acceptance Criteria - All 8 metric descriptions in Act 2 are formatted as unordered list items (`- **metric** -- description`) - The wizard renders them as a `
      ` with `
    • ` elements - No content is lost or changed -- only the formatting prefix is added and paragraph breaks removed - Demo parser integration tests still pass ## Files to Change - `demos/audio-analysis.md` -- Act 2 content (lines 83-114) discovered-from:TF-0MLXF7OU807BWPG6

## Summary

Change the web demo layout so the wizard (demo content) panel and the terminal panel are a vertical split (60% top / 40% bottom) instead of the current horizontal split (60% left / 40% right).

## Current State

In `web/src/index.html`, the `#app` container uses `flex-direction: row` (line 65). `#wizard` has `width: 60%` (line 71) and `#terminal-container` has `width: 40%` (line 78). The wizard has a right border separating it from the terminal.

## Desired Change

Switch `#app` to `flex-direction: column`. Change `#wizard` and `#terminal-container` from width-based to height-based sizing: wizard gets 60% height, terminal gets 40% height. Replace the right border on wizard with a bottom border. Both panels should be full width.

## Implementation

- `#app`: change `flex-direction: row` to `flex-direction: column`
- `#wizard`: change `width: 60%` to `height: 60%`, add `width: 100%`, change `border-right` to `border-bottom`
- `#terminal-container`: change `width: 40%` to `height: 40%`, add `width: 100%`

## Acceptance Criteria

- Wizard content appears on top (60% height), terminal on bottom (40% height)
- Both panels span full width
- Border separates wizard (above) from terminal (below)
- Terminal still functions correctly (xterm.js renders, commands execute)
- Wizard scrolling still works within its panel

## Summary

Rewrite Act 6 ("Batch output to files") in `demos/audio-analysis.md` to present a better use case than "looking for outliers". Instead, frame the scenario as selecting sounds that fit within a target tonal range -- and highlight that this selection process could be fully automated.

## Current State (lines 208-237 in demos/audio-analysis.md)

Act 6 currently frames the use case as saving analysis results for "later processing or integration with other tools." The commentary mentions CI pipelines and downstream features generically. It does not present a compelling, concrete use case.

## Desired Change

Rewrite Act 6 so the narrative is:

1. **Scenario:** You have generated a batch of sounds and want to find the ones that fit within a specific tonal range (e.g., spectral centroid between 600-900 Hz) for use in a game level.
2. **Action:** Use batch output to save JSON analysis files, then show how you could filter them by spectral centroid (or other metrics).
3. **Key message in commentary:** This selection process -- generate a batch, analyze, filter by metrics -- could be fully automated. A script or CI pipeline could generate N seeds, analyze them all, and select only the ones meeting specific metric criteria. No human listening required.

The existing `ls` command and output block can stay. The framing text (blockquote, prose, commentary) needs to be rewritten. Keep the same CLI commands (`toneforge analyze --input ... --output ...`).

## Acceptance Criteria

- Act 6 presents a use case of selecting sounds within a target tonal range
- Commentary explicitly highlights that this selection process could be fully automated
- Existing CLI commands are unchanged
- Demo parser integration tests still pass
- Content reads naturally in the context of the overall walkthrough (Acts 1-5 establish metrics, Act 6 shows practical application)

## Files to Change

- `demos/audio-analysis.md` -- Act 6 content (lines 208-237)

## Summary

The wizard UI splits step content into a left column (description) and right column (commands) when both are present. Currently 31 out of 49 acts across 6 demos have commands but no description text, because the parser routes the only paragraph into the solution field, leaving description empty.

## Root Cause

Steps that follow this pattern get no description:
- blockquote (-> problem)
- single paragraph (-> solution)
- bash code blocks (-> commands)
- commentary blockquote (-> commentary)

Nothing remains for descriptionNodes.

## Fix

Add one or more descriptive paragraphs after the solution paragraph (between or after bash code blocks) so they fall through to descriptionNodes and populate the description field.

## Steps needing fix

- mvp-1.md: Acts 1-4 (4 steps)
- recipe-variety.md: Acts 1-6 (6 steps)
- wav-export.md: Acts 1-4 (4 steps)
- batch-generation.md: Acts 1-8 (8 steps)
- sample-hybrid.md: Acts 4-5 (2 steps)
- sound-stacking.md: Acts 1-3, 5-6 (5 steps)
- audio-analysis.md: Acts 3-4 (2 steps)

## Acceptance Criteria

- All acts with commands also have non-empty description text
- Parser integration tests pass (158 tests)
- All 833 tests pass
- Description text is informative and contextual, not filler

## Summary

Define the classification output schema, the `ClassificationResult` type, and a core `ClassificationEngine` that orchestrates registered dimension classifiers against an `AnalysisResult`.

## Context

This is the foundational feature for Demo 5: Classification (TF-0MLXF7XBI0J0H3P8). It provides the type system and engine that all dimension classifiers plug into. The engine follows the same extensible pattern as the analysis engine (`src/analyze/engine.ts`).

### Player Experience Change

No direct player-facing change. This establishes the classification infrastructure that enables all classification dimensions and CLI commands.

### Reference Documents

- `docs/prd/CLASSIFY_PRD.md` -- Full classification PRD (rule-based subset for this epic)
- `docs/prd/DEMO_ROADMAP.md` (lines 242-284) -- Demo 5 spec
- `src/analyze/types.ts` -- `AnalysisResult` type consumed by the classification engine

## Acceptance Criteria

1. `ClassificationResult` type matches the epic JSON schema: source, category, intensity, texture (string[]), material (string | null), tags (string[]), analysisRef
2. `DimensionClassifier` interface defines a `name` field and a `classify(analysis: AnalysisResult, context?: RecipeContext)` method
3. `ClassificationEngine` accepts registered `DimensionClassifier` instances and runs them in registration order
4. Engine returns a fully populated `ClassificationResult` given an `AnalysisResult` and optional recipe metadata
5. Running `classify()` 10 times with identical inputs produces identical `ClassificationResult` objects (deep equality)
6. Without any registered classifiers, engine returns a result with default/empty values (empty string category, empty arrays, null material)

## Minimal Implementation

- Create `src/classify/types.ts` with `ClassificationResult`, `DimensionClassifier` interface, `RecipeContext` type
- Create `src/classify/engine.ts` with `ClassificationEngine` class (register, classify methods)
- Directly import `AnalysisResult` from `src/analyze/types.ts`
- Unit tests for engine orchestration, determinism (10x identical run), and empty-classifier fallback

## Dependencies

None (Demo 4 analysis types already exist in `src/analyze/types.ts`)

## Deliverables

- `src/classify/types.ts`
- `src/classify/engine.ts`
- Unit tests
data.title: Classification Engine Core & Types

data.description:
## Summary

Implement the category dimension classifier that derives a primary category from recipe name/metadata, with metric-based fallback for unknown sources.

## Context

Part of Demo 5: Classification (TF-0MLXF7XBI0J0H3P8). Category is the primary classification dimension, derived from recipe metadata when available and from analysis metrics when not. The 19 registered recipes span 6 categories: UI, Weapon, Footstep, Creature, Vehicle, Ambient, Character, Impact.

### Player Experience Change

Sounds gain a primary category label (e.g., `weapon`, `footstep`, `ui`) that enables category-based search and filtering.

### Reference Documents

- `src/recipes/index.ts` -- Recipe registry with category and tags per recipe
- `src/core/recipe.ts` -- `RecipeRegistration` interface with `category` and `tags` fields
- Epic description -- Category dimension spec

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Known recipes map to correct categories: `weapon-laser-zap` -> `weapon`, `footstep-stone` -> `footstep`, `ui-scifi-confirm` -> `ui`, `ambient-wind-gust` -> `ambient`, `character-jump` -> `character`, `impact-crack` -> `impact`
2. All 19 registered recipes produce correct lowercase categories when recipe metadata is available
3. When `RecipeContext` is null/undefined, the classifier assigns a category based solely on analysis metrics; a test case with high transient density and short duration produces `impact`
4. Category values are always lowercase strings
5. Classifier is deterministic: same inputs always produce the same category
6. Unit tests cover all 6+ existing category types plus the unknown/no-recipe fallback

## Minimal Implementation

- Create `src/classify/dimensions/category.ts`
- Recipe name parsing: split on `-` to extract category prefix, map to canonical lowercase categories
- Metric-based fallback heuristics for no-recipe inputs using duration, RMS, spectral centroid, attack time
- Unit tests with mock `AnalysisResult` data for each category and fallback scenarios

## Dependencies

- Classification Engine Core & Types (TF-0MM0AJ6R01H1XLVU)

## Deliverables

- `src/classify/dimensions/category.ts`
- Unit tests
data.title: Category Dimension Classifier

data.description:
## Summary

Implement the intensity classifier that maps RMS loudness and peak amplitude to categorical intensity labels.

## Context

Part of Demo 5: Classification (TF-0MLXF7XBI0J0H3P8). Intensity is derived from time-domain metrics produced by the analysis engine. The time-domain extractor provides `rmsLoudness` and `peakAmplitude` values in the `metrics.time` category of `AnalysisResult`.

### Player Experience Change

Sounds gain an intensity label (soft/medium/hard/aggressive/subtle) enabling filtering by energy level.

### Reference Documents

- Epic description -- Intensity dimension spec
- `src/analyze/extractors/time-domain.ts` -- Produces rmsLoudness and peakAmplitude
- `docs/prd/CLASSIFY_PRD.md` Section 5.3 -- Intensity & Energy

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Intensity labels are one of: `soft`, `medium`, `hard`, `aggressive`, `subtle`
2. RMS < 0.05 and peak < 0.1 produces `soft`
3. High RMS (> 0.3) and high peak (> 0.8) produces `aggressive`
4. `subtle` is assigned for low-peak (< 0.15) but moderate spectral centroid (> 1000 Hz) sounds (e.g., ambient textures)
5. Thresholds produce reasonable labels for existing recipe range: UI sounds tend toward soft/medium, weapon/impact sounds tend toward hard/aggressive
6. Classifier is deterministic: same inputs always produce the same intensity label
7. Unit tests cover all 5 intensity levels with representative metric values

## Minimal Implementation

- Create `src/classify/dimensions/intensity.ts`
- Map `metrics.time.rmsLoudness` and `metrics.time.peakAmplitude` (and optionally `metrics.spectral.spectralCentroid` for `subtle`) to intensity labels using threshold ranges
- Unit tests with representative metric values for each intensity level and boundary cases

## Dependencies

- Classification Engine Core & Types (TF-0MM0AJ6R01H1XLVU)

## Deliverables

- `src/classify/dimensions/intensity.ts`
- Unit tests
data.title: Intensity Dimension Classifier

data.description:
## Summary

Implement the texture classifier that derives one or more texture labels from spectral centroid and attack time metrics.

## Context

Part of Demo 5: Classification (TF-0MLXF7XBI0J0H3P8). Texture describes the timbral character of a sound using descriptive labels. It is derived from spectral (spectral centroid) and time-domain (attack time) metrics.

### Player Experience Change

Sounds gain texture descriptors (e.g., `sharp`, `bright`, `warm`) enabling search by timbral quality.

### Reference Documents

- Epic description -- Texture dimension spec
- `src/analyze/extractors/spectral-centroid.ts` -- Produces spectralCentroid
- `src/analyze/extractors/attack-time.ts` -- Produces attackTimeMs
- `docs/prd/CLASSIFY_PRD.md` Section 5.4 -- Texture & Timbre

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Texture is an array of 1-3 labels from: `crunchy`, `smooth`, `noisy`, `tonal`, `harsh`, `warm`, `bright`, `dark`, `sharp`
2. Spectral centroid > 4000 Hz includes `bright` in the texture array
3. Spectral centroid < 500 Hz includes `dark` in the texture array
4. Attack time < 5 ms includes `sharp` in the texture array
5. Attack time > 50 ms includes `smooth` in the texture array
6. Labels are not mutually exclusive: a sound can be `["sharp", "bright"]`
7. Classifier is deterministic: same inputs always produce the same texture array (same order)
8. Unit tests cover at least 5 distinct texture label combinations

## Minimal Implementation

- Create `src/classify/dimensions/texture.ts`
- Map `metrics.spectral.spectralCentroid` and `metrics.time.attackTimeMs` to texture labels using threshold ranges
- Sort texture array alphabetically for deterministic output
- Unit tests with threshold boundary values and combinations

## Dependencies

- Classification Engine Core & Types (TF-0MM0AJ6R01H1XLVU)

## Deliverables

- `src/classify/dimensions/texture.ts`
- Unit tests
data.title: Texture Dimension Classifier

data.description:
## Summary

Implement the material classifier that derives a material label from recipe metadata and spectral characteristics, with graceful omission when no reasonable determination is possible.

## Context

Part of Demo 5: Classification (TF-0MLXF7XBI0J0H3P8). Material is a best-effort dimension -- not all sounds will have a meaningful material label. The classifier assigns material where rules can make a reasonable determination; otherwise the field is null.

### Player Experience Change

Sounds gain a material label (e.g., `stone`, `energy`, `mechanical`) where determinable, enabling material-based search.

### Reference Documents

- Epic description -- Material dimension spec
- `docs/prd/CLASSIFY_PRD.md` Section 5.2 -- Material & Source
- `src/recipes/index.ts` -- Recipe tags contain material hints (e.g., `stone`, `gravel`, `mechanical`)

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Material labels are from: `metal`, `wood`, `stone`, `organic`, `synthetic`, `energy`, `mechanical`, `magical`
2. Recipe metadata provides primary signal: `footstep-stone` -> `stone`, `weapon-laser-zap` -> `energy`, `vehicle-engine` -> `mechanical`, `creature-vocal` -> `organic`
3. When no recipe metadata exists and no clear spectral signal, material is `null` (not guessed)
4. Spectral centroid refines material inference: very high centroid (> 6000 Hz) with metallic recipe hints -> `metal`
5. `footstep-gravel` -> `stone` (gravel is a stone-family material)
6. Classifier is deterministic: same inputs always produce the same material label

## Minimal Implementation

- Create `src/classify/dimensions/material.ts`
- Recipe tag/name pattern matching for known materials (map recipe name segments and tags to material labels)
- Spectral centroid heuristic for secondary refinement
- Return `null` when insufficient signal
- Unit tests for known-material recipes, unknown-material recipes, no-recipe fallback, and null cases

## Dependencies

- Classification Engine Core & Types (TF-0MM0AJ6R01H1XLVU)

## Deliverables

- `src/classify/dimensions/material.ts`
- Unit tests
data.title: Material Dimension Classifier

data.description:
## Summary

Implement the tags classifier that generates contextual use-case tags from category, recipe metadata, and other dimension outputs.

## Context

Part of Demo 5: Classification (TF-0MLXF7XBI0J0H3P8). Tags provide contextual use-case hints that supplement the primary category. When recipe metadata exists, recipe tags are the primary source; otherwise tags are derived from the assigned category and intensity.

### Player Experience Change

Sounds gain contextual tags (e.g., `["sci-fi", "ranged", "laser"]`) enabling tag-based search and filtering.

### Reference Documents

- Epic description -- Tags dimension spec
- `src/recipes/index.ts` -- Recipe registrations include `tags` arrays
- `docs/prd/CLASSIFY_PRD.md` Section 5.5 -- Use-Case Tags

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Tags are an array of 1-5 lowercase strings providing contextual use-case hints
2. When recipe metadata exists, recipe tags are the primary source, filtered to remove duplicates of the category name
3. `weapon-laser-zap` with recipe tags `["laser", "zap", "sci-fi", "weapon"]` produces tags `["laser", "sci-fi", "zap"]` ("weapon" removed as it duplicates the category; remaining sorted alphabetically)
4. When no recipe metadata exists, tags are derived from the assigned category and intensity (e.g., `impact` + `aggressive` -> `["combat", "destruction"]`)
5. Tags array contains no duplicates, all lowercase, alphabetically sorted
6. Classifier is deterministic: same inputs always produce the same tags array

## Minimal Implementation

- Create `src/classify/dimensions/tags.ts`
- Recipe tag passthrough with deduplication, normalization (lowercase), and category-name filtering
- Category-based fallback tag generation lookup table
- Unit tests for recipe-tagged sources, untagged sources, deduplication, and edge cases

## Dependencies

- Classification Engine Core & Types (TF-0MM0AJ6R01H1XLVU)
- Category Dimension Classifier (TF-0MM0AJLM30LMKN8E) -- Tags depend on the assigned category for deduplication and fallback generation

## Deliverables

- `src/classify/dimensions/tags.ts`
- Unit tests

## Summary

Implement `toneforge classify --analysis <dir> --output <dir>` that classifies pre-analyzed sounds in batch, producing one classification JSON file per input.

## Context

Part of Demo 5: Classification (TF-0MLXF7XBI0J0H3P8). This is the first CLI integration feature, wiring all dimension classifiers together into the `toneforge classify` command. Follows CLI patterns established by the `toneforge analyze` command.

### Player Experience Change

Developers can classify a batch of previously analyzed sounds with a single command, producing structured classification JSON files that can be searched and filtered.

### Reference Documents

- Epic description -- CLI command spec
- `docs/prd/DEMO_ROADMAP.md` (lines 269-271) -- CLI commands
- `docs/prd/CLI_PRD.md` Section 5.4 -- `classify` command interface
- `src/cli.ts` -- Existing CLI with hand-rolled arg parser

## Acceptance Criteria

1. `toneforge classify --analysis <dir> --output <dir>` reads all `*.json` files in `--analysis` dir, parses as `AnalysisResult`, and writes one classification JSON per input to `--output` dir
2. Output directory is created if it does not exist
3. Console output shows count of classified files and summary line per file (source, category, intensity)
4. `--json` mode outputs structured JSON array of all classification results to stdout
5. `--format table` shows human-readable table with columns: Source, Category, Intensity, Texture, Material, Tags
6. Invalid/corrupt analysis files are skipped with a warning printed to stderr, not a crash
7. If `--analysis` directory is empty, outputs count of 0 with exit code 0 (not an error)
8. Files flagged as silent by quality flags (`isSilent: true`) are classified with a `silent` warning annotation in the output
9. Exit code 0 on success, 1 on error (missing required args, invalid dir path)
10. Help text (`toneforge classify --help`) follows existing CLI help text conventions
11. Classification output is deterministic: classifying the same analysis directory twice produces identical output files

## Minimal Implementation

- Add `classify` command to `src/cli.ts` argument parser (following the existing `if/else if` dispatch pattern)
- Create `src/classify/index.ts` that constructs and configures the `ClassificationEngine` with all dimension classifiers registered
- Implement batch classification logic: scan dir for JSON files, parse, run engine, write output, print summary
- Table output formatter following `analyze --format table` conventions
- Styled help text
- Integration tests: classify a directory of test analysis JSON files, verify output files, test empty dir, test corrupt file handling

## Dependencies

- Classification Engine Core & Types (TF-0MM0AJ6R01H1XLVU)
- Category Dimension Classifier (TF-0MM0AJLM30LMKN8E)
- Intensity Dimension Classifier (TF-0MM0AJYOF1HKBKMG)
- Texture Dimension Classifier (TF-0MM0AKAKD152P7ZH)
- Material Dimension Classifier (TF-0MM0AKOV61PYWORG)
- Tags Dimension Classifier (TF-0MM0AL3S70OPNW0D)

## Deliverables

- `src/classify/index.ts` (engine assembly)
- Updated `src/cli.ts` (classify command)
- Integration tests

## Summary

Implement `toneforge classify --input <path>` that analyzes and classifies a WAV file (or directory of WAV files) in one step, reusing existing analysis data when available.

## Context

Part of Demo 5: Classification (TF-0MLXF7XBI0J0H3P8). This CLI path provides a convenient end-to-end workflow: given a WAV file, analyze it (using Demo 4's analysis engine) and then classify it, without requiring separate analysis and classification steps.

### Player Experience Change

Developers can classify WAV files directly with a single command, without needing to run analysis separately first.

### Reference Documents

- Epic description -- `--input` CLI path spec
- `docs/prd/DEMO_ROADMAP.md` (line 270) -- `toneforge classify --input <path>`
- `src/audio/wav-decoder.ts` -- WAV file decoder
- `src/analyze/engine.ts` -- Analysis engine

## Acceptance Criteria

1. `toneforge classify --input <path>` accepts a single WAV file path and classifies it end-to-end (decode WAV -> analyze -> classify)
2. `toneforge classify --input <dir>` accepts a directory and classifies all `.wav` files in it
3. Analysis is performed internally using `AnalysisEngine` with all registered extractors
4. If `--analysis <dir>` is also provided, existing analysis data for matching filenames is reused rather than recomputed
5. Outputs classification JSON to `--output <dir>` (defaults to `./classification/`)
6. `--json` and `--format table` work as in batch mode
7. If `--input` path does not exist, prints error message and exits with code 1
8. Non-WAV files in a directory are skipped with a warning
9. Exit code 0 on success, 1 on error

## Minimal Implementation

- Extend classify CLI handler to detect `--input` flag
- Wire WAV decoder -> AnalysisEngine (with all extractors from `src/analyze/index.ts`) -> ClassificationEngine pipeline
- Filename matching logic to check for existing analysis data
- Integration tests with WAV files rendered from recipes

## Dependencies

- CLI Classify -- Batch from Analysis (TF-0MM0ALP3C0PYMX53)
- Demo 4 WAV decoder (`src/audio/wav-decoder.ts`)
- Demo 4 analysis engine (`src/analyze/`)

- Integration tests

## Summary

Implement `toneforge classify --recipe <name> --seed <n>` that renders, analyzes, and classifies a recipe+seed combination in one step.

## Context

Part of Demo 5: Classification (TF-0MLXF7XBI0J0H3P8). This CLI path provides the most convenient classification workflow: specify a recipe and seed, and ToneForge renders the sound, analyzes it, and classifies it -- all in one command. Recipe metadata is available to the classifier, enabling accurate category and tag assignment.

### Player Experience Change

Developers can evaluate classification output for any recipe+seed directly without generating or exporting files first.

### Reference Documents

- Epic description -- `--recipe --seed` CLI path spec
- `docs/prd/DEMO_ROADMAP.md` (lines 269-271) -- CLI commands
- `src/core/renderer.ts` -- Offline renderer (`renderRecipe()`)
- `src/recipes/index.ts` -- Recipe registry with metadata

## Acceptance Criteria

1. `toneforge classify --recipe <name> --seed <n>` renders the recipe with the given seed, analyzes the rendered buffer, and classifies with full recipe metadata context
2. Classification result is output to stdout in default format (summary line) or `--json` / `--format table`
3. Optional `--output <dir>` writes the classification JSON to a file in the specified directory
4. Recipe metadata (category, tags from registry) is passed to the classification engine as `RecipeContext`
5. Invalid recipe name prints error message listing available recipes and exits with code 1
6. Missing `--seed` uses a random seed and prints the chosen seed to console
7. Deterministic: same recipe+seed always produces identical classification output
8. Exit code 0 on success, 1 on error

## Minimal Implementation

- Extend classify CLI handler to detect `--recipe` + `--seed` flags
- Wire: recipe registry lookup -> offline renderer -> analysis engine -> classification engine pipeline
- Pass `RecipeContext` (name, category, tags from `RecipeRegistration`) to classification engine
- Integration tests verifying determinism (classify same recipe+seed twice, compare results)

## Dependencies

- CLI Classify -- Batch from Analysis (TF-0MM0ALP3C0PYMX53)
- Offline renderer (`src/core/renderer.ts`)
- Recipe registry (`src/recipes/index.ts`)

## Deliverables

- Updated `src/cli.ts` (classify `--recipe --seed` handling)
- Integration tests

## Summary

Implement `toneforge classify search` subcommand that searches classification JSON files by category, intensity, and texture filters.

## Context

Part of Demo 5: Classification (TF-0MLXF7XBI0J0H3P8). Search enables developers to find sounds matching specific semantic criteria by scanning classification output files. This is the query side of the classification workflow.

### Player Experience Change

Developers can search for sounds by semantic attributes (e.g., find all `weapon` sounds with `high` intensity and `sharp` texture) without manually browsing classification files.

### Reference Documents

- Epic description -- Search subcommand spec (Success Criterion 6)
- `docs/prd/DEMO_ROADMAP.md` (line 271) -- `toneforge classify search` command
- `docs/prd/CLASSIFY_PRD.md` -- Classification output schema

## Acceptance Criteria

1. `toneforge classify search --category <c>` filters by category (exact match, case-insensitive)
2. `--intensity <i>` filters by intensity (exact match)
3. `--texture <t>` filters by texture (partial match: `--texture sharp` matches `["sharp", "bright"]`)
4. Multiple filters use AND logic: `--category weapon --intensity high` returns only entries matching both
5. `--dir <path>` specifies the classification directory to search (defaults to `./classification/`)
6. `--format table` (default) shows human-readable results with columns: Source, Category, Intensity, Texture, Material, Tags
7. `--json` outputs structured JSON array of matching classification results
8. No matches returns empty result set with exit code 0
9. Invalid/non-existent directory prints error and exits with code 1
10. At least one filter must be provided; no filters prints help and exits with code 1

## Minimal Implementation

- Add `classify search` subcommand to CLI (detect `search` as the second arg after `classify`)
- Scan classification JSON files in the specified directory
- Parse and filter by provided criteria
- Table and JSON output formatters
- Unit tests for filter logic (AND, partial texture match)
- Integration tests for CLI invocation

## Dependencies

- CLI Classify -- Batch from Analysis (TF-0MM0ALP3C0PYMX53) -- classification JSON files must exist to search

## Deliverables

- Updated `src/cli.ts` (classify search subcommand)
- Tests

data.title: Tests, Demos & Observability

data.description:
## Summary

The demo markdown parser (`src/demo/parser.ts`) does not support GFM tables because it uses `remarkParse` without `remark-gfm`. Tables in demo walkthroughs (e.g., `demos/classification.md` Act 2) are parsed as paragraph nodes containing raw pipe-delimited text, which renders as a single long string on the web.

The web wizard renderer (`web/src/wizard.ts`) also uses `.textContent` for solution blocks, which strips any markdown formatting.

## User Story

As a developer viewing a demo walkthrough on the web, I want markdown tables in demo content to render as proper HTML tables, so that structured information (like classification dimensions) is readable and well-formatted.

## Root Cause

1. **Parser**: `unified().use(remarkParse).parse(content)` at line 181 of `src/demo/parser.ts` does not include `remark-gfm`, so GFM table syntax is not recognized as `table` AST nodes.
2. **Renderer**: `solutionText.textContent = step.solution` at line 238 of `web/src/wizard.ts` renders plain text, collapsing any embedded markdown.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. `remark-gfm` is added to the unified pipeline in `src/demo/parser.ts`
2. Table AST nodes are properly classified and serialized in the parser (either as description content or solution content)
3. The web wizard renders tables as proper HTML `<table>` elements
4. The classification demo table in Act 2 renders correctly on the web
5. All existing demo parser tests continue to pass
6. New tests cover table parsing behavior

## Implementation Approach

- Add `remark-gfm` to parser pipeline
- Update `nodesToDescription()` to handle `table` node type, serializing back to markdown table syntax
- In `web/src/wizard.ts`, use `marked.parse()` (already a project dependency) for description/solution rendering, or add table detection to `buildDescriptionElement()`

## Dependencies

data.description:
## Summary

The exploration demo walkthrough (demos/exploration.md) hardcodes run IDs in Acts 5 and 6 (e.g. `explore show --run run-mm0gej70-ffeecb65`). These run IDs are non-deterministic (timestamp + random bytes), so the commands will fail when a user runs the demo since their sweep will produce a different run ID.

## User Story

As a ToneForge user running the exploration demo, I want `explore show` and `explore promote` to accept a `--latest` flag that automatically resolves to the most recent run ID, so I don't need to manually copy run IDs from previous command output.

## Expected Behaviour

- `toneforge explore show --latest` shows the most recent run's details
- `toneforge explore promote --latest --id <candidate>` promotes from the most recent run
- `--latest` and `--run` are mutually exclusive; providing both is an error
- If no runs exist, `--latest` produces a clear error message
- `--json` output works with `--latest`
- Demo walkthrough (Acts 5 and 6) updated to use `--latest` instead of hardcoded run IDs

## Implementation Approach

1. In `src/explore/persistence.ts`: add a `getLatestRunId()` function that returns the most recent run ID (leveraging existing `listRunIds()` which already sorts reverse-chronologically)
2. In `src/cli.ts`: add `--latest` boolean option to `explore show` and `explore promote` subcommands, resolve the run ID from it when `--run` is not provided
3. Update CLI integration tests (`src/cli.explore.integration.test.ts`) to cover `--latest`
4. Update `demos/exploration.md` Acts 5 and 6 to use `--latest`

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `explore show --latest` displays the most recent run
- [ ] `explore promote --latest --id <candidate>` promotes from the most recent run
- [ ] Using both `--latest` and `--run` produces an error
- [ ] Using neither `--latest` nor `--run` produces an error
- [ ] `--latest` with no runs produces a clear error
- [ ] CLI integration tests pass for all `--latest` scenarios
- [ ] Demo parser integration tests pass (195+)
- [ ] Full test suite passes (1061+)
- [ ] Demo file uses `--latest` instead of hardcoded run IDs
data.title: Add --latest flag to explore show and explore promote CLI commands

data.description:
## Investigate and Reduce Latency of Sound Generation

Profile the full `toneforge generate` lifecycle across dev and compiled CLI paths, identify top latency contributors, and implement improvements to reduce time-to-sound.

## Problem Statement

There is a noticeable delay (1-3 seconds) between entering a `toneforge generate` command and hearing the sound. This latency undermines the interactive feel of the tool, especially during exploration workflows where the user is auditioning many seed candidates in rapid sequence.

## Users

**Sound designers and developers** using ToneForge interactively to audition procedural sounds.

### User Stories

- As a sound designer, I want `toneforge generate` to produce sound as quickly as possible after pressing Enter, so that auditioning seeds feels responsive and I can iterate rapidly.
- As a developer, I want a reproducible benchmark suite so I can measure the impact of changes on CLI startup and render latency over time.
- As a contributor, I want profiling data that clearly shows where time is spent in the generate pipeline, so I can make informed decisions about future optimizations.

## Success Criteria

1. Profile the full lifecycle of `toneforge generate` (from process start to audio playback beginning) and document a phase-by-phase breakdown for both the dev (tsx) and compiled (dist/) CLI entry points.
2. Identify and rank the top contributors to latency with quantified timings.
3. Implement at least one measurable improvement to total time-to-sound, or document with evidence why current latency is unavoidable.
4. Provide a before/after timing comparison with reproducible methodology: a shell script for end-to-end CLI timing and a vitest benchmark suite for component-level measurement.
5. All existing tests continue to pass after changes.

## Constraints

- **Platform-agnostic**: Profiling and benchmarks should run on any supported platform (Linux, macOS, Windows). Platform-specific differences should be noted but not block progress.
- **Aggressive changes acceptable**: Architectural changes (esbuild bundling, restructured imports, alternative playback paths) are permitted if justified by profiling data.
- **Full pipeline scope**: The investigation covers the entire path from process start to hearing sound, including CLI startup, module loading, recipe resolution, audio rendering, WAV encoding, and playback device initialization.
- **Independence from realtime work**: This work is independent of the planned real-time engine (TF-0MLYX9DP51U7AQDK / RUNTIME_PRD.md). Improvements should not depend on or block that effort.
- **No specific latency target**: The goal is to investigate and improve what is feasible, not to hit a fixed latency number. Document findings and trade-offs transparently.

## Existing State

- **CLI entry**: `bin/dev-cli.js` registers `tsx/esm/api` for on-the-fly TypeScript compilation, then imports `src/cli.ts`. A compiled entry via `dist/` also exists.
- **Monolithic CLI**: `src/cli.ts` is ~3114 lines; all commands share one file.
- **Eager recipe loading**: All 18 recipes are imported and registered at module load time via `src/recipes/index.ts`. No lazy loading.
- **File-based playback**: Audio is encoded to WAV, written to a temp file, then played by spawning an external process (`paplay`, `aplay`, `ffplay`, `afplay`, etc.). No streaming playback.
- **Minimal instrumentation**: Only ad-hoc `performance.now()` calls around `renderRecipe()` and `renderStack()`. No profiling flags, benchmark suites, or startup timing.
- **Sample-hybrid recipes**: Three recipes (`creature-vocal`, `footstep-gravel`, `vehicle-engine`) load WAV samples from `assets/samples/` via synchronous `readFileSync` + `decodeAudioData`. This disk I/O may add latency compared to pure-synthesis recipes.
- **Sequential stack rendering**: Stack layers render sequentially, not in parallel.
- **Existing timing tests**: A renderer test asserts "under 2 seconds for a 0.5s sound"; a CLI test asserts "completes in under 5 seconds". These are coarse guards, not benchmarks.

## Desired Change

- **Profiling infrastructure**: Instrument the full pipeline with phase-level timing (process start, module load, recipe resolution, context creation, graph build, render, WAV encode, playback launch). Provide a `--profile` flag or similar mechanism.
- **Benchmark suite**: A shell script for end-to-end CLI timing (N iterations with statistical summary) and vitest benchmarks for component-level measurements (renderer, WAV encoder, recipe lookup).
- **Latency reductions**: Implement improvements identified by profiling. Likely candidates include:
  - Lazy recipe imports (load only the requested recipe)
  - esbuild/bundling to eliminate tsx overhead and reduce module resolution time
  - Streaming or direct audio playback to bypass temp-file I/O
  - Parallel or cached operations where safe
- **Documentation**: A written summary of findings, phase timings, implemented mitigations, and remaining bottlenecks with trade-off analysis.

## Risks & Assumptions

### Risks

- **Scope creep**: Performance investigation can expand indefinitely as new bottlenecks are discovered. *Mitigation*: Record opportunities for additional optimizations as separate work items linked to this epic rather than expanding scope.
- **Platform-specific results**: Profiling on one OS may not reflect performance on others (e.g., audio player spawn time, filesystem temp-file speed). *Mitigation*: Note platform in all benchmark results; do not assume cross-platform equivalence.
- **Regression risk from aggressive changes**: Architectural changes (bundling, lazy loading, playback path changes) could introduce subtle regressions. *Mitigation*: Run the full test suite after each change; maintain before/after benchmarks.
- **External dependency bottlenecks**: If `node-web-audio-api` or system audio players are the dominant latency source, improvements may require upstream changes or alternative libraries. *Mitigation*: Document findings and assess alternatives; do not block on upstream fixes.

### Assumptions

- The 1-3 second perceived latency is reproducible and measurable with instrumentation.
- The existing test suite provides sufficient coverage to detect regressions from optimization changes.
- Both the dev (tsx) and compiled (dist/) CLI paths are actively used and worth profiling.

## Related work (automated report)

### Work items

- **CLI startup performance: eliminate Tone.js import and tsx overhead** (TF-0MLW3L9SN041842J, completed) -- Direct prior art. Reduced CLI startup from ~20s to ~2.5s by extracting params into separate files (eliminating Tone.js import at render time) and precompiling to dist/. This work item's investigation should build on those findings and profile what remains of the startup cost.

- **Async Render Pipeline** (TF-0MLYWH5HJ0TF0AAV, completed) -- Changed `buildOfflineGraph` to accept `void | Promise<void>`, adding an `await` in `renderRecipe()`. This async overhead is part of the render phase timing and is required by sample-hybrid recipes that call `decodeAudioData`.

- **Sample-Hybrid Recipes** (TF-0MLYFI4DY12PS034, completed) -- Introduced three recipes (`footstep-gravel`, `creature-vocal`, `vehicle-engine`) that load WAV samples via synchronous `readFileSync` + `decodeAudioData` during graph construction. This disk I/O adds measurable latency compared to pure-synthesis recipes and is a profiling target.

- **Demo 6: Exploration -- Finding the Best Sounds** (TF-0MLXF84591PAUGAJ, completed) -- Has a performance target of 10k seeds under ~2 minutes for batch sweep, which directly depends on per-render latency. Latency improvements from this epic will benefit exploration throughput.

- **Remove startup banner and add version subcommand** (TF-0MLYNPWKT1ISEFXX, completed) -- Removed the unconditional `console.log` banner from `src/index.ts` that ran on every CLI invocation. Minor startup overhead reduction, but demonstrates the pattern of eliminating unnecessary work at startup.

- **Dev Loader Script & Dual Bin Entries** (TF-0MLXLVXVQ17MX7XN, completed) -- Created `bin/dev-cli.js` with tsx registration. References TF-0MLW3L9SN041842J for tsx overhead findings. The dev-cli path is one of the two entry points to profile.

- **Realtime adjustment of parameters** (TF-0MLYX9DP51U7AQDK, open) -- Independent but tangentially related. Plans a real-time playback engine that would bypass the current file-based playback path entirely. Latency improvements here should not depend on or block that effort.

### Repository files

- **`src/core/renderer.ts`** -- Offline render entry point. Calls `buildOfflineGraph()` (now async-aware) and `ctx.startRendering()`. The render phase timing is measured here.
- **`src/recipes/index.ts`** -- Recipe registry with eager imports of all 18 recipes at module load time. A primary optimization target for lazy loading.
- **`src/audio/player.ts`** -- File-based playback via temp WAV file and external process spawn. Covers platform detection, WAV encoding, file write, process spawn, and cleanup. A primary optimization target for streaming/direct playback.
- **`bin/dev-cli.js`** -- Dev CLI entry point with tsx registration. One of the two entry points to profile for startup overhead.
- **`docs/prd/RUNTIME_PRD.md`** -- PRD for the planned real-time playback engine. Defines low-latency playback as a future goal; this latency investigation is independent but findings may inform Runtime design.
- **`docs/prd/COMPILER_PRD.md`** -- PRD for the build/optimization module. Relevant to esbuild/bundling approaches for eliminating tsx overhead and reducing module resolution time.
- **`docs/prd/CLI_PRD.md`** -- CLI interface PRD. Defines the `toneforge <command>` model and automation surface that the `--profile` flag and benchmark tooling must integrate with.
data.title: Investigate and reduce latency of sound generation

data.description:
# Web demo Run button: execute commands sequentially with completion detection

Replace the fixed-delay command staggering in the web demo wizard's Run button with sequential execution that waits for each command to complete before sending the next, providing reliable multi-command step execution.

## Problem Statement

The web demo wizard's Run button fires all commands in a step using a fixed 500ms stagger (`setTimeout` with `i * 500` delay). Commands that take longer than 500ms -- which most `toneforge` commands do -- cause the next command to be injected while the previous one is still running. This results in commands interleaving with output, being lost entirely, or producing garbled results in the terminal.

## Users

**Demo users** stepping through the ToneForge web tutorial.

- As a demo user, when I click Run on a step with multiple commands, I want each command to execute only after the previous one has finished, so that I can see clean output for each command and all commands execute reliably.
- As a demo user, when commands are running, I want the Run button to indicate that execution is in progress, so I know to wait and don't accidentally trigger another run.

## Success Criteria

1. Clicking Run sends the first command and waits for it to complete before sending the next. All commands in a multi-command step execute cleanly in sequence.
2. If a command fails (non-zero exit code), the sequence halts. The Run button reflects this (e.g. re-enables or shows an error state).
3. The Run button is disabled with a changed label (e.g. "Running...") while commands execute, and re-enables on completion or failure.
4. Concurrent runs are prevented: clicking Run on any step while another step's commands are executing is blocked.
5. `handleCommandAudio()` is called for each command at dispatch time.
6. No regression in single-command steps.
7. Existing Vitest unit tests and Playwright E2E tests continue to pass.

## Constraints

- **No marker-based approach**: the implementation must not wrap commands with visible echo markers (e.g. `echo __START__; cmd; echo __END__`). Shell prompt detection, exit-code signalling via WebSocket, PTY idle detection, or a hybrid approach are acceptable.
- **No timeout**: there is no per-command timeout. If a command hangs, the user must manually intervene via the terminal.
- **No Stop/Cancel button**: a button to abort a running sequence is out of scope and should be tracked separately.
- **Visual feedback is button-only**: the in-progress indicator is limited to button state changes (disable + label change). Per-command highlighting or spinners are not required.
- **Server is a pure relay**: currently `web/server/index.ts` does no command parsing. Implementation may add server-side changes if needed (e.g. for exit-code signalling) but should prefer minimal server changes.

## Existing State

- **Run button handler** (`web/src/wizard.ts` lines 249-265): schedules all commands via `setTimeout(() => { terminal.sendCommand(cmd); handleCommandAudio(cmd); }, i * 500)`. No completion detection.
- **`sendCommand()`** (`web/src/terminal.ts` lines 142-148): sends `{ type: "input", data: command + "\n" }` over WebSocket. Returns `void`. No output subscription mechanism.
- **Server** (`web/server/index.ts` lines 102-154): pure PTY-to-WebSocket relay. Receives `input` and `resize` messages. Forwards raw PTY output to the client. No command tracking.
- **E2E tests** (`web/e2e/tutorial.spec.ts`): already implement `waitForCommandCompletion()` (lines 79-96) using shell prompt detection (`$`) and command-specific output patterns ("Rendered", "Tests"). This test-only pattern could inform the production implementation.
- **No unit tests** exist for `wizard.ts` itself.

## Desired Change

1. **Output observation**: add a mechanism to `TerminalController` (or alongside it) that allows the wizard to subscribe to terminal output and detect when a command has completed. The recommended approach is shell prompt detection or exit-code signalling over WebSocket. The E2E test helper `waitForCommandCompletion()` provides a reference pattern.
2. **Sequential execution**: replace the `setTimeout` stagger in the Run button handler with an async loop that sends a command, awaits its completion, then sends the next.
3. **Failure detection**: detect non-zero exit codes and halt the sequence, updating the button to indicate failure.
4. **Button state management**: disable the Run button and change its label (e.g. "Running...") while commands are executing. Re-enable on completion or failure.
5. **Global run guard**: prevent concurrent execution from multiple steps. If a run is in progress, other Run buttons should be disabled or clicks should be no-ops.
6. **Audio**: ensure `handleCommandAudio(cmd)` is called at command dispatch time for each command (same as current behaviour, but now sequenced).

## Risks & Assumptions

- **Shell prompt detection fragility**: if using prompt detection, the approach depends on a known prompt pattern (e.g. `$ `). Custom shell prompts, multi-line prompts, or commands that output `$ ` in their output could cause false positives. Mitigation: make the prompt pattern configurable and test with the demo's default shell environment.
- **Exit code detection may require server-side changes**: depending on the chosen approach, detecting non-zero exit codes may require the server to parse or wrap commands, adding complexity. Assumption: the implementer will choose the approach that minimizes server changes while reliably detecting failures.
- **No timeout means potential indefinite hang**: without a per-command timeout, a hanging command will leave the wizard in a "Running..." state indefinitely. Assumption: this is acceptable for a demo environment where the user has terminal access to intervene manually. A Stop/Cancel button (out of scope) would mitigate this in a future iteration.
- **Scope creep**: additional UX enhancements (per-command progress, stop button, retry button, command output panels) should be recorded as separate work items linked to this one, not added to this scope.
- **E2E test timing**: the Playwright E2E tests currently rely on the existing Run button behaviour. The tests should continue to pass or be updated to work with sequential execution. Assumption: the E2E `waitForCommandCompletion()` pattern is compatible with the new approach since it already waits for completion.

## Related work (automated report)

**Work items:**

- **Stepped Wizard UI** (TF-0MLX7D5DO0OXTNXY, completed) -- Implemented the wizard component in `web/src/wizard.ts` including the `buildCommandsElement()` function and the Run button handler that this feature modifies. The 500ms stagger pattern was introduced here. Direct predecessor.
- **Terminal Emulator (xterm.js + node-pty)** (TF-0MLX7CQ3F0F3A6ZS, completed) -- Implemented `web/src/terminal.ts` including `sendCommand()` and the WebSocket relay in `web/server/index.ts`. This feature needs to extend the `TerminalController` interface with output observation. Direct predecessor.
- **Web demo for CLI demo** (TF-0MLW1WRKR17QIHC7, completed) -- Parent epic for the web demo. This feature is a follow-on enhancement to the web demo's command execution model.
- **Dev server crashes on EADDRINUSE** (TF-0MLYI74BU1I33ZJN, completed) -- Introduced the Vite dev server proxy and `.port` file coordination used by the WebSocket connection. Changes to the server relay may interact with this port coordination.
- **Web UI: Demo content and terminal as vertical split (60/40)** (TF-0MM05JK1J1V6SC58, completed) -- Changed the wizard/terminal layout. The button state changes in this feature should be consistent with the current layout.

**Repository files:**

- `web/src/wizard.ts` -- Contains the Run button handler (lines 249-265) and `buildCommandsElement()` (lines 231-268). Primary file to be modified.
- `web/src/terminal.ts` -- Contains `sendCommand()` (lines 142-148) and WebSocket handling. Needs output observation mechanism added.
- `web/server/index.ts` -- PTY relay (lines 102-154). May need changes if exit-code signalling approach is chosen.
- `web/e2e/tutorial.spec.ts` -- E2E tests with `waitForCommandCompletion()` (lines 79-96). Reference pattern for prompt-based completion detection; tests must continue to pass.
- `web/test/server.test.ts` -- Server integration tests including Run button simulation (lines 149-189). Tests must continue to pass.
- `web/test/commands.test.ts` -- Validates demo step structure and command format. Tests must continue to pass.
data.title: Web demo Run button: execute commands sequentially with completion detection

## Pipeline Profiling Infrastructure

Add a `--profile` CLI flag that instruments and reports phase-by-phase timing for the full `toneforge generate` lifecycle.

### User Story

As a developer, I want to run `toneforge generate --recipe <name> --seed <n> --profile` and see a phase-by-phase timing breakdown on stderr, so I can identify exactly where time is spent in the generate pipeline.

### Context

This is the foundational feature for the latency investigation epic (TF-0MM0W4YUZ0QF26DG). All other optimization features depend on the profiling data this feature produces.

**Key source files:**
- `bin/dev-cli.js` — dev CLI entry point (earliest timing anchor)
- `src/cli.ts` — CLI command parsing and dispatch
- `src/core/renderer.ts` — offline render pipeline
- `src/audio/player.ts` — WAV encode + playback

### Existing State

Only ad-hoc `performance.now()` calls exist around `renderRecipe()` and `renderStack()`. No profiling flags, no phase-level breakdown, no startup timing.

## Acceptance Criteria

- `toneforge generate --recipe <name> --seed <n> --profile` prints timing breakdown to stderr covering: process start, module load, CLI parse, recipe resolution, context creation, graph build, render, WAV encode, file write, playback launch.
- Each line follows the pattern `<phase>: <N>ms` on stderr, making output machine-parseable.
- Works on both dev (`bin/dev-cli.js`) and compiled (`dist/`) paths.
- No performance impact when `--profile` is not specified.

### Minimal Implementation

- Record `process.hrtime.bigint()` at process entry (earliest possible point in `bin/dev-cli.js` and `dist/` entry).
- Add timing checkpoints at each pipeline phase in renderer, player, and CLI.
- Collect timings in a shared profiling context; print summary to stderr at exit when `--profile` is set.
- Add a unit test verifying profiling output format.

### Deliverables

- `--profile` flag implementation
- Profiling infrastructure code (timing collection module)
- Tests verifying output format and no-impact when disabled
data.title: Pipeline Profiling Infrastructure

data.description:
## End-to-End Benchmark Suite

Create a reproducible benchmark script and coarse vitest benchmarks to measure CLI latency with statistical rigor, enabling before/after comparisons.

### User Story

As a developer, I want a benchmark script I can run to get statistically meaningful timing data for the full `toneforge generate` pipeline, so I can confidently measure the impact of optimization changes.

### Context

Part of the latency investigation epic (TF-0MM0W4YUZ0QF26DG). Depends on Feature 1 (Pipeline Profiling Infrastructure, TF-0MM0YTYLE0BN0YR9) for `--profile` data.

**Key source files:**
- `src/core/renderer.ts` — render timing target
- `src/audio/wav-encoder.ts` — WAV encode timing target
- `src/recipes/index.ts` — recipe lookup timing target

## Acceptance Criteria

- A shell script (`scripts/benchmark-cli.sh`) runs N iterations of `toneforge generate` on both dev and compiled paths, reporting mean, median, min, max, and p95 times.
- Vitest bench files cover render time, WAV encode time, and recipe lookup time.
- Running the same benchmark twice on the same machine produces results within 20% variance.
- Benchmark results are reproducible (fixed recipe, fixed seed, documented platform).
- Instructions for running benchmarks are documented in the benchmark script header.

### Minimal Implementation

- Shell script: loop N iterations, capture wall-clock time, compute stats (via `bc` or Node helper).
- Vitest `.bench.ts` files for renderer, WAV encoder, and recipe registry lookup.
- Run benchmarks on current code to establish baseline.

### Deliverables

- `scripts/benchmark-cli.sh`
- Vitest bench files (renderer, WAV encoder, recipe lookup)
- Baseline results recorded in work item comments
data.title: End-to-End Benchmark Suite

data.description:
## Lazy Recipe Loading

Replace eager import of all 18 recipes at module load with on-demand loading of only the requested recipe, reducing startup time.

### User Story

As a sound designer, I want `toneforge generate` to start faster by only loading the recipe I requested, so that auditioning sounds feels more responsive.

### Context

Part of the latency investigation epic (TF-0MM0W4YUZ0QF26DG). Currently `src/recipes/index.ts` eagerly imports all 18 recipe modules (and their dependencies including sample loaders) at module load time. This means even a simple `toneforge generate --recipe ui-scifi-confirm` pays the cost of importing all 17 other recipes.

**Key source files:**
- `src/recipes/index.ts` — recipe registry with eager imports (1754 lines, 18 recipes)
- `src/core/recipe.ts` — RecipeRegistry class
- `src/core/renderer.ts` — calls `registry.getRegistration()`

### Existing State

All 18 recipes are imported and registered at module load time. Three sample-hybrid recipes (`creature-vocal`, `footstep-gravel`, `vehicle-engine`) additionally load WAV samples from `assets/samples/` via synchronous `readFileSync` + `decodeAudioData`.

## Acceptance Criteria

- `src/recipes/index.ts` no longer eagerly imports all recipe modules at load time.
- Only the recipe specified by `--recipe` is imported during a `generate` command.
- All existing tests pass without modification (or with minimal adaptation).
- Module-load phase time is reduced by at least 20% compared to baseline, as measured by `--profile`.
- Recipe listing commands (`toneforge list`) still enumerate all available recipes without loading their implementations.

### Minimal Implementation

- Refactor `RecipeRegistry` to accept lazy factory descriptors (recipe name -> dynamic `import()` thunk).
- `src/recipes/index.ts` registers recipe metadata (name, param getter) eagerly but defers `buildOfflineGraph`/factory imports to first use.
- Update `renderer.ts` to `await` the lazy resolution.
- Verify determinism is preserved (same seed = same output).

### Deliverables

- Refactored recipe registry with lazy loading support
- Updated `src/recipes/index.ts` with deferred imports
- Updated renderer to handle async recipe resolution
- Tests verifying lazy loading behavior and determinism
data.title: Lazy Recipe Loading

data.description:
## Direct-to-Device Playback

Add a lightweight playback path that pipes PCM/WAV audio directly to the system audio player's stdin, bypassing temp file write and cleanup.

### User Story

As a sound designer on WSL/Linux, I want audio playback to skip writing a temp file to disk, so that I hear the generated sound faster after rendering completes.

### Context

Part of the latency investigation epic (TF-0MM0W4YUZ0QF26DG). The current playback path in `src/audio/player.ts` encodes WAV, writes to a temp file via `writeFile()`, spawns an external player process pointing at the temp file, waits for completion, then deletes the temp file. This adds filesystem I/O latency that could be eliminated by piping audio data directly to the player's stdin.

The developer's primary environment is WSL with PulseAudio (WSLg socket at `/mnt/wslg/PulseServer`).

**Key source files:**
- `src/audio/player.ts` — current file-based playback implementation
- `src/audio/wav-encoder.ts` — WAV encoding (produces Buffer)

### Existing State

Audio is encoded to WAV Buffer via `encodeWav()`, written to a temp file (`tmpdir()/toneforge-*.wav`), played by spawning an external process (`paplay`, `aplay`, `ffplay`, `afplay`), then the temp file is cleaned up. Platform detection and player selection logic is already in place.

## Acceptance Criteria

- When a compatible player is detected (e.g., `paplay`, `aplay`, `ffplay`), audio is piped via stdin instead of writing a temp file.
- Falls back to existing temp-file path if stdin piping is not supported or fails.
- Playback produces identical audible output to the existing temp-file path.
- Playback-phase time (from WAV encode start to audio heard) is reduced compared to temp-file path, as measured by `--profile`.
- Works on WSL with PulseAudio (the developer's primary environment).
- If stdin piping fails or the player does not support it, the system falls back to temp-file playback without error.

### Prototype / Experiment

Test `paplay` stdin piping on WSL with PulseAudio socket to validate feasibility before full implementation. Success threshold: audio plays correctly when WAV data is piped to `paplay` stdin.

### Minimal Implementation

- Detect players that accept WAV/PCM on stdin (e.g., `paplay` accepts WAV on stdin by default, `aplay` accepts stdin, `ffplay -i pipe:0`).
- Encode WAV to a Buffer (already done by `encodeWav`), pipe to player `stdin` instead of writing to disk.
- Add fallback: if stdin pipe fails, fall back to temp-file path with a warning.
- Integration test: verify playback completes without temp file creation.

### Deliverables

- Updated `src/audio/player.ts` with stdin piping logic
- Fallback path to temp-file when piping is unsupported
- Tests verifying both paths
data.title: Direct-to-Device Playback

data.description:
## Compiled CLI Build Optimization

Optimize the compiled `dist/` build to minimize module resolution and startup overhead, bringing compiled path performance closer to optimal.

### User Story

As a developer, I want the compiled CLI to start as fast as possible so that the production/installed experience has minimal latency overhead from module resolution.

### Context

Part of the latency investigation epic (TF-0MM0W4YUZ0QF26DG). The compiled `dist/` path eliminates tsx overhead but may still have significant module resolution time due to many individual .js files. esbuild single-file bundling could eliminate per-file resolution.

Prior art: TF-0MLW3L9SN041842J reduced startup from ~20s to ~2.5s by adding the compiled path. This feature investigates further reduction.

**Key source files:**
- `dist/` — compiled CLI output
- `package.json` — build scripts
- `tsconfig.json` — TypeScript compilation config
- `docs/prd/COMPILER_PRD.md` — relevant PRD for build/optimization approaches

## Acceptance Criteria

- Compiled CLI (`dist/`) startup time is profiled and documented.
- If esbuild bundling or tree-shaking reduces startup time, it is implemented.
- If esbuild bundling does not reduce startup time by a measurable margin, the feature is closed with documented evidence.
- The compiled path does not regress in functionality (all tests pass via `dist/` entry).
- Before/after comparison is recorded using the benchmark suite.

### Minimal Implementation

- Profile `dist/` startup to identify module resolution overhead using `--profile` flag.
- Evaluate esbuild single-file bundling for the CLI entry point (eliminates per-file module resolution).
- If beneficial: add esbuild build step, update `dist/` entry point, verify all tests pass.
- If not beneficial: document findings and rationale for keeping current build.

### Deliverables

- Build configuration changes (if applicable)
- Before/after timing measurements
- Updated build scripts (if applicable)
- Documentation of findings in work item comments
data.title: Compiled CLI Build Optimization

## Parallel Stack Layer Rendering

If profiling shows sequential stack rendering is a significant bottleneck, implement parallel rendering of independent stack layers.

### User Story

As a sound designer, I want multi-layer stacks to render faster by processing independent layers in parallel, so that complex stacked sounds have lower latency.

### Context

Part of the latency investigation epic (TF-0MM0W4YUZ0QF26DG). Stack layers currently render sequentially. If profiling reveals this is a significant contributor to latency for multi-layer sounds, parallel rendering via `Promise.all()` could reduce render time.

This feature is explicitly conditional on profiling data. If stack rendering is not a significant bottleneck, the feature closes with documented findings.

**Key source files:**
- `src/core/renderer.ts` — render pipeline
- `src/cli.ts` — stack rendering orchestration (look for `renderStack`)

## Acceptance Criteria

- Stack renders with multiple layers show measurable improvement in render time.
- Audio output is deterministic and byte-identical to sequential rendering for the same seed.
- All existing stack tests pass.
- If profiling shows stack rendering is NOT a significant bottleneck, this feature is closed with documented evidence as "not needed — documented."

### Minimal Implementation

- Profile multi-layer stack rendering to quantify sequential overhead using `--profile` flag.
- If justified: render independent layers in parallel via `Promise.all()`, merge results.
- Ensure deterministic ordering of parallel results to preserve byte-level reproducibility.
- Add benchmark for multi-layer stack render time.

### Deliverables

- Parallel render implementation (conditional on profiling data)
- Benchmark results for multi-layer stacks
- Documentation of findings in work item comments

## Findings Summary and Comparison

Compile profiling results, document all implemented optimizations with before/after measurements, and record remaining bottlenecks and trade-off analysis.

### User Story

As a contributor, I want a clear summary of what was measured, what was optimized, and what bottlenecks remain, so I can make informed decisions about future performance work.

### Context

Final wrap-up feature for the latency investigation epic (TF-0MM0W4YUZ0QF26DG). This feature depends on all other features being complete (or closed with documented findings).

## Acceptance Criteria

- Before/after timing comparison is recorded in the epic work item comments with reproducible methodology.
- Each implemented optimization has a quantified impact measurement.
- Remaining bottlenecks are identified with trade-off analysis.
- Any optimization opportunities not pursued are recorded as separate work items.

- Compile phase-by-phase timing comparison using `--profile` output.
- Document platform (WSL/Linux), Node.js version, and methodology.
- Record findings as structured comments on the epic work item.
- Create new work items for any discovered optimization opportunities not addressed in this epic.
